112000:
May 15, 2025

The Inference Inflection: Where Real-Time AI Meets Real-World
Opportunity
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

While everyone was busy debating tariffs, AI inference quietly had its breakout moment.
While markets fixated on trade headlines and the daily churn of social media noise, a far more important macro trend was
again quietly gaining momentum: AI. Long before tariffs took center stage, it was the release of DeepSeek R1 on
Inauguration Day that truly set things in motion—triggering a wave of positioning unwinds driven by fears of capex spend
and forcing investors onto the defensive by the time we reached Liberation Day.
While everyone sat on their hands waiting for the uncertainty to decline, capex fears were proven wrong and the tone of
corporate Q1 earnings calls shifted decisively—from talk of model training and GPU arms races to the explosive growth of
AI inference and capacity constraints. This is the real-time side of artificial intelligence, where trained models move from
experimentation into action—powering decisions, automation, and scaled deployment.
Microsoft’s Q3 FY25 earnings underscored the magnitude of this shift: Azure processed over 100 trillion tokens that
quarter, including 50 trillion in March alone—a fivefold year-over-year surge. It marks a definitive pivot away from the
research-heavy phase of AI and into the age of inference at scale.
And we’re just getting started. With the upcoming rise of AI agents, AI-native phones and PCs, and embodied AI like selfdriving platforms and humanoid robots, inference demand is poised to go parabolic. The implications for semiconductors,
memory, networking, power, and edge compute are massive. If Q1 earnings told us anything, it’s that scalable inference
infrastructure has officially arrived.
Key Statements Highlighting the Inference Boom
The statements below, sourced from Q1 2025 earnings calls and summaries, emphasize the rapid ascent of inference as
AI’s next frontier and affirm that the time to act is now:
1. Microsoft (MSFT) – Q3 FY25 (January 2025)
“Inference usage has grown fivefold year-over-year, with over 100 trillion tokens processed, including a record 50 trillion
in March alone, reflecting the shift toward real-time AI applications driving unprecedented demand for our Azure AI
infrastructure.”
– Satya Nadella, CEO
2. Amazon (AMZN) – Q1 2025 (April 2025)
“Most of the spend today in AI has been on training. But when you get to scale of the production applications for AI, it
flips. The overwhelming majority of the spend is actually on inference—the predictions of the model.”
– Andy Jassy, CEO
3. NVIDIA (NVDA) – Q4 FY25 (February 2025)
The CEO noted that inference has surpassed training as the primary AI workload in many applications, fueling demand
for GPUs and networking solutions to support real-time processing in cloud and edge environments.
4. Meta Platforms (META) – Q1 2025 (April 2025)
The CEO highlighted that inference workloads now drive the majority of AI compute spending, as deployed models

 power real-time features across products, requiring significant data center infrastructure upgrades.
5. Qualcomm (QCOM) – Q1 CY2025 (February 2025)
The CEO emphasized that AI inference at the edge is experiencing rapid growth, particularly in automotive and IoT,
with chips enabling real-time processing for a multi-billion-dollar market.
6. Alphabet (GOOG) – Q1 2025 (April 2025)
“Our leadership in AI continues to drive extraordinary results in Google Cloud, where inference workloads are scaling
rapidly, fueled by customer demand for real-time generative AI applications.”
– Sundar Pichai, CEO
7. AMD (AMD) – Q1 2025 (April 2025)
“What we’re seeing is that with these new reasoning models, inferencing is becoming more important. There’s also a
shift toward more distributed inferencing, and I think that plays to our strengths.”
– Lisa Su, CEO
8. Equinix (EQIX) – Q1 2025 (May 2025)
The CEO stated that the surge in AI inference workloads is driving unprecedented demand for low-latency data center
capacity, with hyperscalers expanding footprints to support real-time AI applications.
9. Micron Technology (MU) – Q3 FY25 (March 2025)
“Our HBM3E memory is seeing incredible demand for AI inference, where high bandwidth and low latency are critical,
driving record data center revenue this quarter.”
– Sanjay Mehrotra, CEO
10. Intel (INTC) – Q1 2025 (April 2025)
The CEO noted that inference workloads are fueling demand for specialized AI chips and networking solutions, with
their Gaudi accelerators and Ethernet offerings enabling real-time processing in data centers and edge environments.
11. Ciena Corporation (CIEN) – Q1 FY25 (March 2025)
The CEO noted that surging demand for AI connectivity is driving significant order growth from hyperscale customers,
with high-bandwidth optical solutions enabling real-time AI applications like inference in data centers.
Inference has moved from the sidelines to the center of AI infrastructure. The following companies are broken down by
strategic area—chips, memory, networking, data centers, cloud, and edge/embodied AI—offering a roadmap for investors
seeking exposure to the next major wave of AI-driven demand.
AI Compute & Inference Chips
These companies provide GPUs, accelerators, or edge inference chips:
1. NVIDIA (NVDA) – The market leader in both training and inference GPUs; inference now driving data center growth.
2. AMD (AMD) – Seeing momentum with MI300 and distributed inference models.
3. Intel (INTC) – Gaudi AI chips gaining traction; Ethernet and inference-optimized silicon part of roadmap.
4. Qualcomm (QCOM) – Dominant at the edge (phones, automotive, IoT); custom inference chips for low-power devices.
5. Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) – FPGA-based low-power inference plays at the edge.
Memory & Storage
These names benefit from rising HBM, DRAM, and edge memory demand:
1. Micron Technology (MU) – Leader in HBM3E and low-latency DRAM used for inference workloads.
2. Western Digital (WDC) – Drives inference-related data center storage needs.

2

 3. Seagate Technology (STX) – Supports growing edge/cloud storage requirements tied to inference.
Networking & Optics
Enabling inference via ultra-low latency, high-bandwidth connectivity:
1. Ciena Corporation (CIEN) – Optical backbone provider; strong AI data center build exposure.
2. Arista Networks (ANET) – Top player in high-speed networking for hyperscalers deploying inference.
3. Marvell Technology (MRVL) – Custom silicon and optical interconnects powering inference networks.
4. Broadcom (AVGO) – Dominant in networking chips, PCIe, and switching—core enabler of inference scale-out.
Data Centers & Infrastructure
Inference demand is straining and expanding data center infrastructure:
1. Equinix (EQIX) – Hyperscaler growth and latency-sensitive inference demand driving record colocation needs.
2. Digital Realty (DLR) – Benefiting from real-time AI workloads shifting inference into new facilities.
3. Vertiv (VRT) – Power and cooling infrastructure for inference-heavy data centers.
AI-Optimized Cloud Providers
Large-scale inference customers and beneficiaries of usage-based cloud economics:
1. Microsoft (MSFT) – Azure’s 100-trillion token quarter is the proof point.
2. Alphabet (GOOG) – Cloud inference usage expanding fast.
3. Amazon (AMZN) – AWS seeing a shift toward inference-driven demand.
Edge & Embodied AI Enablers
The next leg of inference: robotics, smart devices, FSD, and more:
1. Ambarella (AMBA) – Edge AI chips used in automotive and vision systems.
2. ON Semiconductor (ON) – Inference-capable image sensing and power semiconductors for AI-native systems.
3. NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) – Automotive AI and edge inference control.
4. Synaptics (SYNA) – AI-powered interfaces and inference at the edge (phones, consumer, IoT).
You’ll notice that many of the names on this list are semiconductor companies—and yes, they’re positioned to benefit the
most. If you build an equal-weight basket of these stocks, the historical price chart closely resembles the SOX
(Semiconductor Index). Each company addresses a different part of the inference buildout, but there’s no escaping the
massive demand ahead for memory, networking, power, and edge compute as the AI race accelerates.
Many of these names are currently priced as if we’re heading into a cyclical downturn—partly due to tariff noise and capex
fears stemming from DeepSeek. But ironically, DeepSeek has actually accelerated the shift toward inference over training,
which is exactly why Satya Nadella’s recent comments were so important. He called DeepSeek a Jevon’s Paradox
moment—and so far, he’s been right for increased demand.
As we move into the next phase—embodied AI—inference demand is only going to grow.

May 18, 2025

Stocks Rally, But Investors Are Paralyzed
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

Investors seem paralyzed as this stock market rally rolls on. The S&P 500 has been up five days in a row and 16 of the
last 19 since the Treasury Secretary’s dinner where he signaled de-escalation with China. Now, the index sits just below
all-time highs—yet positioning remains hesitant, and the focus is still on the wrong risks.
In this week’s video, I explain why headlines about tariffs and recession are distracting investors from the much bigger
structural shifts unfolding beneath the surface.
The U.S. economy, driven by services and top-heavy consumption, shows no signs of traditional recession. Jobless
claims are steady, earnings are strong, and capital markets have rebounded sharply. Meanwhile, semiconductors and AI
—especially inference, the real-time use of trained models—are now the driving force behind margin expansion and
upside surprises. Inference isn’t a buzzword—it’s the engine behind the AI spending boom that’s fueling the next leg of
tech performance.
But the bigger shift as a consequence of the global trade war is structural: a weakening U.S. dollar, capital repatriation
from Asia, the democratization of AI and rising long-term rates threaten America’s dominance in long-duration assets like
tech, private equity and venture capital. At the same time, Bitcoin is quietly gaining strength as a borderless hedge in this
transition, with institutional adoption accelerating alongside surging stablecoin use.
AI and Bitcoin are no longer fringe. Together, they’re forming the new infrastructure of a financial system that’s rapidly
diverging from the past.
Key Timestamps & Highlights
(00:00 – 04:00) | Market Recovery & Tariffs Shift
Markets staged a rapid “I-bottom” recovery with five straight up days.
Tariffs are fading as the main story; strategic decoupling is underway.
(04:00 – 08:00) | Hedge Fund Capitulation & Currency Headwinds
Hedge funds triggered the second-largest short covering ever.
U.S. investors are up, but foreign investors are hurt by the strong dollar.
(08:00 – 12:00) | Semis, AI, and Sentiment Reset
Semiconductor stocks, especially Nvidia, lead the rally amid AI tailwinds.
Sentiment and positioning have not completely reset, clearing the way for upside.
(12:00 – 16:00) | The Soft Data Rebound vs. Hard Data Stability
Economic soft data (like surveys and earnings momentum) is rebounding.

 Jobless claims remain stable—no evidence of recession in the labor market.
(16:00 – 20:00) | Inflation, Deficits, and Dollar Pressures
Deficits, not tariffs, are the real inflation threat as stimulus grows.
Repatriation of capital from Asia and Moody’s downgrade signal deeper risks.
(20:00 – 24:00) | Capital Flows & The Asian Currency Breakout
The Taiwan dollar and other Asian currencies are surging—big signal.
The real risk: U.S. rates rise while the dollar weakens—a break from past cycles.
(24:00 – 30:00) | AI’s Inference Boom & Capex Illusion
AI inference is exploding—100 trillion tokens processed in Q1.
S&P margins are inflated by delayed Capex expenses while revenues (like Nvidia’s) are booked instantly.
(30:00 – End) | Bitcoin, Stablecoins & The Future of Capital Markets
Bitcoin is consolidating near all-time highs, adoption quietly accelerating.
Stablecoins and AI-native startups are building a new capital structure outside traditional markets.
Watch Here: https://youtu.be/TkJCsFnhHTs

112519:
May 19, 2025

The AI Remedy: Rethinking the Fiscal Deficit in an Exponential Age
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

I was listening to a podcast this morning where two macro thinkers I respect were discussing the U.S. fiscal deficit and
how the recent Moody’s downgrade had reignited a familiar debate: which asset class bears the brunt of this obvious
imbalance? As is often the case, their conclusion pointed to equities as the likely casualty. While these were thoughtful
and experienced voices, I wasn’t surprised by their conclusion—just reminded of a recurring pattern. Many macro
strategists seem predisposed to viewing equities as overvalued, largely because they rely on traditional business cycle
frameworks that assume the next recession is always lurking just around the corner, triggered by mounting
macroeconomic imbalances. Equities always seem to be the biggest risk.
But as I argued in last week’s paper, recessions in today’s digital economy aren’t what they used to be. We now live in a
world where a structurally large fiscal deficit—once a hallmark of a deep recession—exists with a resilient economy. We
have a Federal Reserve armed with four letter acronym liquidity facilities at the first sign of systemic stress to prevent
contagion. The old playbook from a fiscal and monetary perspective is outdated. This is the math argument against a
recession. And I say math intentionally—because ever since I read Daniel Pink’s A Whole New Mind, I’ve tried to remain
open to creativity in math problems. In the book, Pink argues that we’re using the wrong side of the brain entirely—
clinging to left-brain logic in a world where the future is shaped by intuition, creativity, and nonlinear change. To some
degree, it can be interpreted as a version of garbage in-garbage out.
The timing of that book was critical to how I began viewing macroeconomics. It came out in 2005, and I read it around the
same time the iPhone was launched. That’s why I like writing papers that draw on right-brain creativity to explore the
future—even for something that appears as mathematical and rigid as the fiscal deficit. When change happens in a linear
fashion—like a child growing up in front of your eyes—you often don’t notice it happening. But now, change is anything
but linear.
Adding to this structural shift in monetary and fiscal policy is the rise of a flexible labor force—fueled by smartphones,
software platforms, and the normalization of remote work—which allows businesses to scale up or down more fluidly,
muting the kinds of job shocks that once signaled downturns. Building on that idea—and continuing my belief that
provocation cuts both ways—I want to flip the script and propose a new path toward reducing the fiscal deficit to 3%.
Rather than focusing solely on cutting spending or raising taxes, we should seriously reconsider the denominator: nominal
GDP.
What if, over time, GDP is simply being measured too low? If we properly accounted for the exponential value created by
modern technology—especially AI—nominal GDP could potentially be revised significantly from its current size. AI allows
us to do more with fewer inputs at an accelerating rate, and this compounding effect could lift measured output
substantially in the coming years. In other words, solving the deficit might not require austerity—it might require better
measurement.
This mismeasurement has broader implications, particularly in how we interpret market signals. Many investors and
economists continue to expect a recession because traditional indicators—consumer confidence, ISM prints,
delinquencies, and commercial real estate stress—continue to flash red. One of the most obvious data points that should
have made people more cautious about mismeasurement came after one of the fastest rate hikes in history back in 2022:
shockingly, nonfinancial corporate business net interest payments fell to near record lows, largely because tech
companies held massive cash balances and carried far less debt than older industrial firms in the opposite position.

 The equity market also tells this story in other ways, depending on where you look. Small-cap stocks, represented by the
Russell 2000, have significantly underperformed both the S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 since 2007—and the divergence has
widened since the pandemic. That isn’t noise. It’s the market discounting a tale of two economies: one rooted in old
models, debt, and rate sensitivity—lacking the culture, technology infrastructure, or balance sheet to adopt AI; the other
flush with capital, embedded in the digital stack, and structurally positioned to benefit from AI. The Mag 7 tech giants—
which dominate the SPX and NDX—are less exposed to interest rates and far more equipped to deploy AI at scale. So
while economists continue to call for a broad downturn, markets are increasingly reflecting a structural shift—not a cyclical
one.
This divergence between the two economies isn’t limited to the private sector. It’s just as stark in government. Much of
Washington still operates in the analog world—defined by legacy systems, outdated metrics, and bloated administrative
overhead. It’s a government built around the assumptions of industrial-era GDP, not one that reflects the speed,
complexity, or capabilities of the digital economy. The inefficiency is systemic, and it’s costly. From the IRS to the
Department of Veterans Affairs, antiquated systems slow everything down—verification, payment processing, fraud
detection, procurement, benefits management. This is where AI could deliver enormous returns: by modernizing
workflows, replacing manual processes with automation, and enabling real-time data analytics that hold bureaucracies
accountable. In a sense, the public sector is the small-cap equivalent of the federal balance sheet—sluggish, debt-laden,
and struggling to adapt—while the AI-powered private economy is racing ahead with margin expansion and productivity
gains. That gap is now showing up in the fiscal deficit—and AI may be the only tool fast enough to help close it and the
numbers are large based on estimates.
One of the largest and fastest-growing components of federal spending is healthcare—specifically, Medicare and
Medicaid, which reached record levels in 2024. AI is poised to help here too, not just in administrative efficiency but in
transforming the health outcomes themselves. Through advancements in diagnostics, personalized medicine, AI-driven
drug discovery, and platforms like AlphaFold, we are moving rapidly toward a world where chronic disease is both more
preventable and more manageable. And it goes further. Ray Kurzweil, the Godfather of the Singularity, has recently
reiterated his forecast that humans could achieve biological immortality by 2030 through nanobots and cellular repair. As
radical as it sounds, progress in understanding why we age—and how to slow or even reverse it—is accelerating,
powered by AI. The implications for Medicare and Medicaid spending are profound. Longer healthspans, fewer
hospitalizations, and delayed end-of-life care would significantly reduce entitlement costs—changing the shape of the
federal budget. Again, the most important part of this is the forecasted time due to AI rapidly growing at a pace impossible
to comprehend.
If we are indeed measuring GDP too low while also failing to anticipate dramatic reductions in future spending, then we’re
essentially living in a Truman Show–like environment—one where economists, investors, and policymakers are reacting to
a distorted reality. Models built on historical data begin to fail, and efforts to forecast asset behavior or assess government
solvency become misguided. It’s no wonder many still expect equities to follow a traditional recessionary cycle—because
in parts of the economy, recession signals are clearly flashing. But the U.S. economy is increasingly bifurcated. On one
side, you have the modern economy—capital-light, AI-levered, debt-free, and margin-rich. On the other, you have smaller,
rate-sensitive firms that are struggling to adapt. Understanding this divergence is key to understanding why the deficit
story isn’t as simple as it seems.
This challenge speaks to a deeper shift in how we need to think. In A Whole New Mind, Daniel Pink argued that the world
is increasingly being shaped not by left-brain, analytical thinking, but by right-brain, creative, intuitive thinking. It’s not just
about spreadsheets anymore—it’s about storytelling, synthesis, and seeing patterns that don’t yet exist. Even AI doesn’t
know about these possible changes unless you ask the right question. This is the future and takes creativity and
dreaming, not extrapolating the past. The economic revisions we constantly see are not simply technical updates; they are
evidence that traditional models are struggling to keep up with a world that no longer moves in predictable, linear patterns.
Younger generations, raised in digital chaos and fluid career paths, seem more attuned to this reality: the future is
uncertain, and old tools won’t work. Many of the most important technology builders of the past 30 years—Steve Jobs, Bill
Gates, Elon Musk (for his PHD), Mark Zuckerberg—either dropped out of college or never finished. That isn’t a rejection
of knowledge, but of systems that constrain creativity. For truly creative thinkers, school and memorizing history can
flatten original thought and suppress the kind of nonlinear imagination needed to build the future. This also touches the
conversation around Bitcoin, but that’s a discussion for another time. For now, one thing is clear: whether macro thinkers

2

 want to admit it or not, AI is rewriting the rules. And the fiscal deficit, like everything else, is going to be redefined. Assets,
as always, will reflect this ahead of the macro podcasts I listen to daily.

112967:
May 23, 2025

The Art of Unlearning: Lessons from Josh Waitzkin in the Age of AI
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

“In the long run, the sharpest weapon of all is a kind of resilient adaptability.”
We all have songs that instantly transport us back to specific memories. For me, certain books do the same—and The Art
of Learning by Josh Waitzkin is one of them. For those of you who don’t know already, Josh was the child chess prodigy
that served as the story for the 1980s movie, Searching for Bobby Fischer. I first read it in the aftermath of the Great
Financial Crisis, a time when I was seeking out ideas that would challenge what I thought I knew and help me open my
mind to the future. I recently referenced Daniel Pink’s A Whole New Mind. It was another book that shaped my thinking
about the brain, psychology and learning at the time. When I find a topic that fascinates me, I dig deep and back the,
books were real time information. The 1990s were dubbed “the decade of the brain,” and the years that followed brought a
wave of literature on learning, cognition, and adaptability. Among them all, Waitzkin’s work stands out as the most
impactful.
Years later, I wrote a paper titled The Art of Unlearning, where I argued that in a world defined by exponential change, true
learning requires unlearning—an intentional process of letting go of outdated frameworks and remaining open to new
ideas. Most people are trying to interpret the present at the speed of a bike, but we’re now moving at 200 miles per hour—
where the present and future blur together, and by the time you’ve made sense of ‘now,’ it’s already behind you. To stay in
the race, you have to unlearn as quickly as you learn—because anchoring to the past is the brake that keeps you from
adapting in real time. This philosophy has guided my journey in using, analyzing, and imagining the future of AI and the
profound ways it will transform our lives. It has also shaped my views on artificial intelligence’s impact on economics,
business cycles, and the future of capital structures. Waitzkin’s book, often viewed as a manual for peak performance,
offers something deeper: a framework for dismantling what we know to make space for what we need to learn next.
After achieving national fame as a chess prodigy, Josh Waitzkin made the unexpected decision to walk away from the
game entirely. He shifted his focus to mastering a new discipline: the martial art of Tai Chi, specifically the competitive
form known as push hands. It was a profound transition—not just between skills, but between mindsets. Tai Chi push
hands is rooted in balance, adaptability, and sensing change in real time—making it a fitting analogy for how we must now
engage with AI. Imagine riding the same escalator every day. You know its speed and rhythm. But one day, you step onto
it and it accelerates dramatically. Your brain, conditioned by past experience, is unprepared. You lose balance. But if you
had anticipated the change—if you had trained for unpredictability—your adjustment would be smoother. This is the world
we now live in: an AI-driven economy where the rules are shifting faster than our cognitive defaults can process. In this
environment, the ability to “learn how to learn” is no longer optional—it’s a survival skill. And this kind of learning isn’t
additive; it’s subtractive. It begins with letting go—of ego, of control, and of what we think we already know. For now, the
acceleration of AI is easy to overlook because it’s mostly happening in software. But that won’t last. As AI becomes
embodied—in humanoids, in full self-driving systems—the visual, physical presence of change will hit much harder.
Preparing for that shift will challenge even the most adaptable among us. And more importantly, I don’t believe you can
truly understand the present—especially in markets and macroeconomics—without understanding AI.
“Growth comes at the point of resistance.”
Waitzkin makes it clear that true learning doesn’t happen when everything is going well—it begins when we hit internal
walls. In The Art of Learning, he shares how trying to apply his chess-trained precision to Tai Chi initially led to repeated
failure. His muscle memory, rooted in logic and calculation, clashed with the fluid, reactive demands of a contact-based

 martial art. But those failures weren’t dead ends—they were thresholds. Each one exposed the limitations of his old
knowledge and opened the door to something new.
This lesson has never been more relevant. As AI reshapes entire industries, professionals are discovering that what once
made them successful may now hold them back. The marketer who relied on gut instinct must now master algorithmic
targeting. The lawyer trained on precedent must now partner with predictive models. The doctor who memorized protocols
must now work alongside diagnostic AI. These aren’t just skill shifts—they’re identity shifts. And they require the humility
to face resistance, not as failure, but as a cue to evolve.
“We have to be willing to let go of our sense of knowing, to step into the unknown with humility.”
One of the greatest barriers to AI is not technical—it’s psychological. It’s one thing to talk about AI’s potential, but it’s
another to personally confront what it asks of us: to begin again. For professionals who have spent years or decades
mastering their field, this can feel like erasure. It’s not just about learning a new tool. It’s about stepping into unfamiliar
territory without a map and accepting that what made you successful before may no longer apply.
Adoption requires the courage to start from zero. And it’s precisely here that many get stuck—not because they lack the
intelligence to understand AI, but because they resist the vulnerability of not being the expert anymore. As Waitzkin
reminds us, the beginner’s mindset isn’t a weakness—it’s a superpower. Children learn quickly because they haven’t yet
hardened around fixed beliefs—they’re open, curious, and unafraid of failure. Their brains are wired for experimentation,
not preservation. In a time of exponential change, we must cultivate that same openness. The ability to adopt is the ability
to let go. The ones who thrive will not be those who cling to expertise, but those who embrace the humility of starting over.
“Making smaller circles.”
Waitzkin’s concept of “making smaller circles” is about simplifying without losing depth—distilling complex ideas into
internalized, intuitive knowledge. This doesn’t happen quickly. He emphasizes a long-term, organic process of
transformation—slow, often invisible progress made through repetition and refinement. In an era dominated by AI, this
lesson feels countercultural.
The speed of AI adoption tempts us to look for quick hacks or plug-and-play solutions. But meaningful fluency in AI—true
integration—requires time and curiosity. It’s not enough to prompt a model. We must understand why it responds the way
it does, how data moves through it, and where its limitations lie. This is pattern recognition over time. It’s learning how to
think with machines, not just use them. Waitzkin’s call to deepen, rather than rush, offers a blueprint for developing AI
intuition in a world obsessed with shortcuts.
“Learn the macro from the micro.”
Waitzkin discovered that letting go of structured systems and embracing spontaneity was essential to his growth. In Tai
Chi, this meant responding to force not with force, but with flow—meeting tension with softness. This looseness, once a
weakness in his mind, became a strength. He learned to trust in responsiveness over rigidity.
The same lesson applies in the age of AI. These systems are probabilistic, not rule-based. They work best when guided,
not commanded. Analysts, teachers, and business leaders who cling to linear thinking—who expect tidy inputs to produce
tidy outputs—struggle with AI. Success comes not from control, but from calibration. From testing, iterating, adjusting. The
professionals who thrive are those who embrace the ambiguity, not resist it. Just like Waitzkin, they learn that flexibility—
not precision—is what leads to mastery in complex systems.
There is where AI excels. As an example of attacking the impact of AI to economics and the argument I make about
recessions not existing, let’s use Okun’s Law as something to use AI to learn flexibility. Okun’s Law has long been a
foundational concept in macroeconomics because it provides a simple, intuitive link between growth and employment. It
suggests that when the economy grows faster than its potential, unemployment falls, and when growth slows, joblessness
rises. Policymakers have used it for decades to estimate how much GDP growth is needed to bring down unemployment
rates or, conversely, to gauge how a slowdown might impact the labor market. It’s been a key tool for forecasting
recessions, setting interest rates, and designing fiscal stimulus.

2

 So here is a simple Q&A I will do with ChatGPTQ: Does Okun’s Law still hold in a world of AI-driven productivity?
A: Not in the way it used to. Okun’s Law is built on the assumption that economic growth and employment are tightly
linked—that you need more workers to produce more output. But AI breaks that relationship. As machines and algorithms
increasingly perform tasks once handled by humans, we can now generate more economic output without increasing
labor—and in some cases, while reducing it. This means GDP can rise even as employment stagnates. In an AI-driven
economy, productivity gains are no longer necessarily tied to job creation. Instead, they are increasingly tied to capital
investment in technology, data infrastructure, and software—decoupling growth from traditional labor dynamics. In this
way, AI doesn’t just change how we work—it challenges the very models we’ve used to understand the economy for
decades.
And now anotherQ: Has labor flexibility changed since the rise of the smartphone?
A: Dramatically. The smartphone, combined with cloud software and communication tools, has untethered work from fixed
locations and traditional hours. This shift gave rise to the gig economy, remote work, and a new class of contingent labor.
Today, businesses can scale labor up or down with far greater speed and precision than in past economic cycles. This
flexibility dampens the traditional signals of downturns—like mass layoffs or unemployment spikes—because companies
can trim hours, shift to freelancers, or automate tasks rather than eliminate full-time jobs. In this way, the smartphone era
has redefined labor dynamics, contributing to why recessions may appear softer or more fragmented in data, even when
structural adjustments are underway.
“The obstacle is the path.”
Perhaps the most difficult part of unlearning is the emotional toll. Waitzkin built his identity around mastery—first in chess,
then in structured thought. Stepping into a more chaotic martial discipline forced him to not only rebuild technique but to
confront the ego built on expertise. For many navigating today’s AI transition, this feels familiar.
Credentials, status, and institutional knowledge are being disrupted by technologies that can outperform humans in
narrow domains. That sense of loss—of status, of control, of predictability—is real. But it’s not the end of value; it’s the
beginning of reinvention. As Waitzkin learned, embracing chaos doesn’t mean giving up—it means evolving. And it’s in
that discomfort that our most human capabilities—creativity, intuition, judgment—can reemerge with new power.
“The art of learning is to become resilient, not perfect.”
Waitzkin’s final and perhaps most essential message is this: learning is not about flawless execution—it’s about durable
transformation. It’s not a ladder you climb, but a cycle you move through again and again. In the AI era, this perspective is
liberating. No one will master it all. Tools will evolve, systems will break, paradigms will shift.
What matters most is resilience—the ability to begin again without ego, to update without fear, and to approach each
iteration with curiosity. We’re entering a future where every form of expertise will be tested. But if we adopt the mindset
Waitzkin models—humble, open, resilient—we’ll remain capable not only of surviving disruption, but of thriving within it.

113023:
May 25, 2025

Debt, Disruption, and Digital Gold: A Market at a Crossroads
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

In this week’s market update, the focus shifted sharply to the bond market as long-term U.S. Treasury yields surged
toward levels not seen since the Global Financial Crisis. This move has reignited systemic risk concerns reminiscent of
the UK’s 2022 “Liz Truss moment.” The video highlights how rising yields, deteriorating foreign demand, and an
unsustainable fiscal trajectory—exacerbated by growing deficits and political resistance to austerity—are converging to
challenge global capital markets. Discussions around capital controls, remittance taxes, and liquidity support suggest that
policymakers are beginning to contemplate more interventionist approaches, which could mark a shift in regime from
market-determined interest rates to more overt financial repression. Long-duration and illiquid assets, particularly in
private equity and venture capital, remain structurally vulnerable under this backdrop.
Meanwhile, artificial intelligence is fundamentally reshaping the macroeconomic landscape. Profit margins in AI-leveraged
sectors continue to expand, decoupling equity performance from traditional rate sensitivity. The rapid rise in productivity,
paired with fiscal incentives like bonus depreciation, is helping to offset the negative impact of higher rates and delaying
the onset of recessionary pressures. In this environment, Bitcoin is framed as the primary beneficiary of growing distrust in
fiat systems—fueled by central bank constraints, capital flow restrictions, and the accelerating digital asset infrastructure.
With institutional adoption rising and Bitcoin now outperforming most other assets, the case is made for it emerging as the
next global reserve alternative in an era defined by policy distortions and exponential technological change.

113276:
May 28, 2025

The Intelligence Era Arrives: Disney and the Next Frontier of AI
Productivity
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

“We keep moving forward, opening new doors, and doing new things, because we’re curious and curiosity keeps leading
us down new paths.” – Walt Disney
Last week at the 2025 Google I/O developer conference, Google unveiled Veo 3, its most advanced AI video generator to
date. Introduced during the keynote address, Veo 3 showcased a range of groundbreaking features, including integrated
audio generation, improved prompt adherence, and photorealistic video realism—all created from simple natural language
inputs. But unlike prior AI milestones that centered on language, Veo 3 signals something far more profound: the transition
from AI as a chatbot to AI as a system that begins to think, sense, and reason like a human. This is no longer about
generating text—it’s about simulating how we experience the world through sight, sound, and movement, all processed
contextually and simultaneously. Veo 3 creates immersive, sensory-rich environments by aligning audio, visual, and
motion cues with a deep understanding of scene dynamics. It represents a foundational shift in the development of
intelligent systems, setting the stage for embodied reasoning—where AI agents don’t just respond, but interpret and act
based on a rich, multi-sensory awareness of their environment.
But the excitement around Veo 3 also exposes the underlying constraint in this new frontier: infrastructure. While some
investors had speculated that breakthroughs like DeepSeek might reduce the demand for semiconductors and data
centers, Veo 3 puts that notion to rest. Its staggering compute requirements—driven by the complexity of generating
coherent video with synchronized audio, realistic motion physics, and temporal continuity—highlight the widening gap
between AI’s capabilities and the global supply of GPUs. This is why Veo 3 currently limits users to 8-second clips and is
available only through Google’s $250-per-month AI Ultra plan. If made freely accessible, the current global inventory of
GPUs would be insufficient to meet demand. But this next phase of AI—marked by sensory synthesis and embodied
reasoning—is also broadening the semiconductor stack itself. What began with language-based chatbots running on
general-purpose GPUs now requires high-bandwidth memory, optical interconnects, specialized accelerators, and custom
silicon designed for multimodal processing. The infrastructure challenge is no longer just about scaling compute—it’s
about reengineering the entire AI hardware pipeline to support machines that see, hear, and think like humans.
In this new phase of AI—where real-time, multimodal inference enables intelligence to blend seamlessly into physical and
digital workflows—the most profound productivity gains won’t be found in the next app or language model, but in
organizations that rely on large human workforces, spatial interaction, and rich storytelling. Companies like Disney are
uniquely positioned to benefit. With operations that span immersive physical environments, high-cost creative production,
global customer interaction, and real-time media delivery, Disney is a microcosm of the broader U.S. economy. It operates
not just in pixels and data but in theme parks, retail supply chains, live performances, and branded experiences. These
require the kind of intelligent adaptation, personalization, and automation that Veo 3-like models now make possible.
Disney has not only anticipated this shift—it has spent the last several years investing heavily in AI tools across its parks,
studios, streaming platforms, and merchandising systems. The company offers a lens into how AI’s next chapter—
centered on inference, not just training—will transform labor-intensive, guest-facing, and emotionally resonant businesses
and impact GDP for the country.
Disney has not only anticipated this moment but has positioned itself as one of the leading legacy companies embracing
the agentic, inference-driven AI future. Since 2020, its leadership has been openly bullish about AI’s role in boosting
productivity, enhancing creativity, and personalizing customer experiences. “AI may, in fact, be the most powerful

 technology that our company has ever seen,” said CEO Bob Iger at Disney’s 2025 annual shareholder meeting, adding
that it is already helping Disney become more efficient and “we’re only just beginning to deploy it for those purposes.”
Iger’s tone has consistently reflected both excitement and caution—emphasizing the need to safeguard creative integrity
and intellectual property while embracing AI’s potential to “create efficiencies and ways for us to provide better service to
customers.” For Disney, the imperative to use AI is not abstract—it’s operational and people-centered. AI has become a
tool to help cast members work smarter, deliver richer guest experiences, and improve the efficiency of storytelling itself.
The result is a clear trend already emerging in financial metrics, including revenue per employee and margins, as Disney
begins to harness the real-world power of AI now entering the inference era.
Disney’s studio division—spanning Pixar, Marvel Studios, Lucasfilm, and Walt Disney Pictures—stands to gain immediate
and dramatic productivity enhancements from AI-powered video generation technologies like Veo 3. The advent of realtime, multi-modal inference—where AI can generate entire scenes from text and image prompts—has direct implications
for Disney’s blockbuster-heavy production model. Traditional VFX-laden films like Avengers: Endgame can cost upwards
of $10,000 to $20,000 per second of finished footage. In contrast, Veo 3’s estimated cost ranges from $0.39 to $0.75 per
second. Assuming a 90-minute film with a 30:1 footage-to-final ratio, Veo 3 could cut production costs to as low as
$63,000 for the same output—a staggering shift. While not a wholesale replacement for studio-quality final production, this
enables previsualization, rough cuts, and secondary content generation at scale. Disney is already leveraging AI across
ILM and Pixar to streamline VFX, automate rotoscoping, and reduce render times. The striking de-aging of Harrison Ford
in Indiana Jones 5 shows not only time and cost savings, but a deeper potential: actors no longer need to age out of roles.
With permission and protection, AI-created digital doubles can keep characters and franchises alive indefinitely. “AI is just
another tool in the toolbox,” said ILM’s Rob Bredow, emphasizing its role in accelerating—not replacing—the creative
process. From creative development to post-production and marketing, generative AI is becoming a force multiplier for
Disney’s storytelling engine.
In the world of streaming and digital media, personalization is not just a convenience—it’s a competitive advantage.
Disney’s adoption of AI in its Direct-to-Consumer platforms (Disney+, Hulu, ESPN+) and media channels is central to
transforming passive content delivery into a deeply personalized entertainment ecosystem. With AI-enabled
recommendation engines, Disney can dynamically curate content based on viewing behavior, age group, mood, and even
franchise affinity—delivering experiences that feel tailored to each individual viewer. Unlike its competitors, Disney has a
brand relationship that begins at childhood. AI allows that bond to grow alongside the audience, adapting
recommendations from Moana and Frozen to Marvel and Star Wars, keeping the brand relevant as preferences evolve.
Advertising, too, benefits from inference at scale. Disney’s unified ad platform enables precise targeting across streaming
and broadcast, using viewer data, location, and even offline experiences (like park visits) to match the right message with
the right person. Netflix has long set the standard for streaming personalization and churn reduction, but AI gives Disney
the tools to close that gap—by increasing content discovery, improving retention, and enhancing monetization through
smarter, more relevant ads. With a broader ecosystem that spans physical and digital experiences, Disney has unique
data inputs to fuel these algorithms and convert them into sustained viewer engagement and revenue. The outcome is a
media ecosystem that not only entertains but intelligently adapts to the individual—deepening loyalty and lifetime value.
Nowhere is Disney’s fusion of technology and storytelling more visible—or more operationally complex—than in its Parks
& Experiences division. As sprawling physical ecosystems that function as both entertainment destinations and small
cities, Disney’s parks are ripe for transformation through AI. With more than $60 billion committed to expansion over the
next decade, AI and robotics are central to scaling experiences without scaling labor linearly. “Storytelling through
characters” is the future, said Josh D’Amaro, Chairman of Parks, Experiences & Products, as he introduced free-roaming
robotic prototypes at SXSW—including rollerblading droids and somersaulting animatronics. These characters do more
than entertain—they create new forms of interaction while reducing dependency on cast-heavy shows. Platforms like
Genie and Genie+ optimize guest flow in real time, improving park-wide logistics and guest satisfaction. AI enables
predictive maintenance, energy savings, labor scheduling, and dynamic pricing. Disney is also beginning to use AI agents
in call centers and customer service interactions—automating trip planning, dining reservations, and hotel bookings to
reduce wait times and frustration. By shifting these tasks to responsive, intelligent systems, Disney improves cost
efficiency while also increasing customer satisfaction before guests even arrive. Once inside the park, AI helps manage
the guest journey dynamically—guiding visitors to shorter lines or underutilized areas, and even dispatching performers in
real time to redirect crowds. These capabilities turn logistical friction into surprise-and-delight moments. Notably, Disney
has faced persistent labor shortages in recent years, especially in hospitality and entertainment roles across its parks,

2

 which made the development of a long-term AI strategy not just optional but essential. Every layer of the park becomes
more adaptive, more efficient, and more personalized—not only enhancing the magic, but doing so with smarter margins.
While less headline-grabbing than cinematic breakthroughs or robotic theme park characters, Disney’s Consumer
Products and Licensing business is perhaps the most quietly lucrative area for AI-driven transformation. This segment—
which generates billions in high-margin revenue from merchandise, licensing, and brand collaborations—relies on
understanding trends, forecasting demand, and rapidly delivering what consumers want. AI models now parse social
media sentiment, park behavior, and streaming engagement to inform product design and inventory allocation. That
insight helps Disney avoid overproduction and stockouts, improving margins while enhancing the customer experience.
Generative design tools allow product teams to iterate on new concepts instantly—from apparel to toys—accelerating
time-to-market and expanding personalization. AI also enables dynamic pricing and product recommendations in ecommerce, while scanning for counterfeit goods using computer vision. Even in licensing, AI is helping simulate product
concepts and identify underutilized IP opportunities. This quiet layer of intelligence ensures that Disney extracts full value
from its creative output while offering fans exactly what they want, when they want it, and often in personalized form.
Until now, the investment narrative around artificial intelligence has largely been confined to infrastructure: the data
centers, semiconductors, and hyperscalers powering large models and AI training. But Veo 3 and Disney’s AI playbook
signal a turning point—one that shifts attention from compute capacity to people productivity. Disney is not a technology
company in the traditional sense, yet it is quickly becoming one of the most important case studies in how AI will integrate
into the human-facing economy. Whether it’s storytelling, service, merchandising, or logistics, Disney’s operations mirror
the broader U.S. labor market: high-touch, creatively demanding, logistically complex, and deeply consumer-centric. That
makes its early AI gains—from content acceleration to experiential personalization—not just impressive, but instructive.
Disney is becoming a microcosm of how the American economy will absorb and adapt to inference-driven intelligence. It’s
not just about making robots or faster chips—it’s about merging thought, creativity, and experience into physical spaces
and services. As AI agents move from abstract ideas to operational teammates, companies like Disney show how this
technology can amplify—not replace—human ingenuity. And as AI leaves the data center and enters the real world—from
theme parks to retail shelves—the productivity gains we’ve seen in theory will begin to manifest across sectors. Disney’s
AI transformation is not the end of a cycle. It’s the beginning of a new one—where intelligence, embedded everywhere,
becomes the operating system for how we live, work, and create. As Disney embraces this new era of AI-powered
creativity, personalization, and embodied intelligence, it does so not merely to optimize operations, but to build a future
where technology enhances wonder and storytelling for generations to come—because, as Walt himself reminded us, “our
greatest natural resource is the minds of our children.”

113641:
June 1, 2025

Why Veo 3 Signals an AI Job Disruption—and a New Global Economic
Order
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

In this week’s video, I explore how the return of tariff uncertainty, fears over capital controls, and global trade realignment
is reshaping markets and accelerating key investment themes. Despite a strong May for equities, especially tech, broader
investor sentiment remains cautious. As mega-cap tech leads, small caps continue to lag.
I revisit Stephen Miran’s playbook of using tariffs and currency realignment as tools to protect U.S. competitiveness,
warning that if multilateral fixes fail, unilateral capital controls could emerge. This rising economic nationalism is mirrored
abroad, with the UK pressuring pension funds to invest domestically and Asia potentially repatriating U.S. dollar assets. In
this shifting landscape, I see Brazil as an example of a potential beneficiary, thanks to its critical rare-earth minerals and
potential tailwinds from a weaker dollar.
The real economic story, however, lies in the exponential acceleration of AI adoption, particularly the surge in inference
workloads. OpenAI’s Veo 3 marks a turning point, revealing massive inference GPU needs and data center constraints as
AI video generation explodes. Meanwhile, Demis Hassabis and Sergey Brin forecast AGI by 2030 and major medical
breakthroughs extending human lifespan and the implications for the fiscal deficit.
Despite job-loss fears, I go through why labor shortages, not unemployment spikes, will define the near-term landscape,
with AI acting as “free labor” to boost profit margins. Bitcoin, in my view, has become the “hurdle rate for innovation,” as
governments explore strategic reserves, tokenization of real-world assets, issuance of bonds linked to Bitcoin and 401(k)
access, pointing to deeper institutional adoption amid a regime shift in capital and monetary frameworks.
⏱ Timestamps
(00:00 – 04:45): May was the strongest month for equities since 2023. Tech leads, but investor sentiment remains
cautious.
(05:40 – 08:30): Tariff rhetoric returns. Stephen Miran’s framework includes capital controls if diplomacy fails—global
signs of a capital war appear.
(08:30 – 11:00): Rare-earth minerals put Brazil and Greenland in focus. Stronger Asian FX and repatriation flows
would support Brazil further.
(11:59 – 15:55): AI hardware super-cycle builds. OpenAI’s Veo 3 creates GPU “meltdown” conditions. Power
infrastructure becomes key.
(17:50 – 21:50): AGI by 2030? Hassabis and Brin believe so—plus potential for life-extension medical breakthroughs.
(23:30 – 26:45): Dario Amodei warns of 10–20% job losses. Visser counters: AI = free labor for corporations, not mass
unemployment.
(26:45 – 29:57): High rates choke small caps. Russell 2000 flat since 2015; many firms risk obsolescence.

 (30:55 – 36:28): Bitcoin as the innovation benchmark. Strategic reserves, tokenization pilots, and 401(k) access hint
at large-scale inflows.

113864:
June 3, 2025

Brazil’s Moment: The Trade of the Embodied AI Era, Dollar Weakness,
and Global Trade Reordering
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

Executive Summary
Because of my time living in Brazil, I am always looking for top-down reasons to get long it when it is off investor’s radar.
Coming into this year, I was very focused on Brazil being an AI trade due to the rise of embodied AI and the commodity
and mineral needs for it. However, now that the U.S. is committed to restructuring global trade and with it, capital flows, it
is apparent the dollar weakness and Asia repatriation story is real, it’s time to bring out the Brazil long trade idea as it
benefits significantly from both.
Brazil stands at the epicenter of a powerful global reordering. While most investors remain preoccupied with tariffs, U.S.
recession risk, inflation, and long-duration bond risks, Brazil has quietly become one of the most compelling macro
opportunities of 2025.
This trade is driven by three major secular tailwinds:
1. The structural weakening of the U.S. dollar and diversification of global capital flows,
2. The next phase of artificial intelligence defined by hardware, power, and materials, not software and
3. A global push to secure critical minerals due to the trade war between China and the U.S., where Brazil is rising fast as
a strategic supplier.
So far this year, Brazil’s currency has strengthened, bonds are offering one of the world’s highest real yields, and its equity
market is pushing through major multi-year resistance. Yet the trade is still under-owned, largely because the biggest
fears in markets right now, Brazil’s fiscal deficit and inflation have overshadowed its structural improvements.
What investors are missing is that these very fears have historically been defused during past cycles of dollar weakness
and commodity strength precisely the environment that is now more likely to take shape driven by the global macro
tailwinds.
I. From Dollar Risk to Yield Opportunity
Under the Trump administration’s aggressive trade policies, the dollar is facing renewed pressure as countries
increasingly look to de-dollarize. Brazil is at the forefront of this shift. Bilateral trade with China is now settled in BRL and
CNY, and the BRICS bloc is building frameworks for local-currency settlements.
This transformation is coinciding with a return to emerging market (EM) carry trades:
Brazilian 10-year government bonds yield over 15%, far outpacing inflation.
These yields reflect not crisis, but opportunity enabled by credible monetary policy, disinflation progress, and a
proactive central bank.

 A 1% drop in the DXY has historically translated into a 2.5–3.0% appreciation in high-yield EM currencies like the BRL.
In addition, over the last 22 years it has also led to MSCi Brazil rising over 4% for each 1% drop in the DXY.
While fiscal sustainability remains a concern with Brazil’s gross public debt is expected to reach 92% of GDP this year,
these worries are not new. Historically, periods of dollar weakness have supported Brazil’s fiscal repair by boosting capital
inflows, compressing imported inflation, and improving debt affordability.
II. AI’s Next Chapter: A Hard Asset Supercycle
The AI story has shifted. We are now leaving behind the software era and entering a capital- and energy-intensive
hardware phase:
Inference models and humanoid robotics,
AI-native mobile devices and full self-driving fleets,
Data centers consuming more electricity than EVs.
This shift is driving a new commodity supercycle centered on:
Lithium, nickel, copper, graphite, and rare earths—all of which Brazil possesses in abundance.
Brazil has:
The second-largest reserves of rare earths, nickel, and graphite,
A growing lithium export sector,
And massive untapped copper and manganese resources.
Brazil now sits at the center of the beginning of the embodied AI era, with the materials the world needs to scale.
III. The Power Behind AI and Brazil’s Strategic Clean Energy Edge
AI is no longer just about silicon, it’s about energy. Power is becoming the next major bottleneck for global compute
expansion.
AI-driven data centers are forecast to use 1,500 TWh of electricity by 2030.
Brazil generates over 84% of its electricity from renewables: hydro, wind, and solar.
This makes Brazil one of the most attractive countries on earth for AI infrastructure buildout. Microsoft, ByteDance, and
others are building or expanding in Brazil, supported by government tax relief and favorable energy costs.
IV. The Fears: Fiscal and Inflation but the Cycle May Help
The two biggest headline concerns in Brazil right now are:
1. A fiscal deficit projected at 8.5% of GDP in 2025, and
2. Inflation expected at 5.65%, above the 3% target.
But these concerns are cyclical, not structural. History shows they are often best addressed during periods of dollar
weakness and commodity booms. In 2003–2007, Brazil’s fiscal deficit shrank below 2% of GDP while debt/GDP dropped
from ~60% to ~45%.
V. Technical Breakout: The Charts Agree
2

 John Roque’s Technical Read:
USDBRL has broken below its 40-week average and is targeting 4.60.
EWZ has reversed a false breakdown and is pushing into breakout territory.
IBOV shows a long-term cup-and-handle, with targets at 154,000 and 174,000.
My Takeaway (Elliott Wave Perspective):
As someone who studies Elliott Wave patterns, my personal read is as follows:
Elliott Wave: A 13-year bear cycle appears complete with a textbook 5-wave decline in BRL/USD.
Momentum: Monthly MACD divergence + flattening histogram is a reliable historical bottom signal.
Bias: Tactical bullish. A long-term reversal thesis is building. Confirmation comes on a break above 0.1850.
VI. The Trade Setup
Ways to express this trade:
FX: Long BRL/USD or EMFX baskets with Brazil overweight
Equities: Long EWZ, or direct Brazilian exposure through banks, utilities, or infrastructure
Rates: Long Brazil local-currency bonds (NTNs or local bond ETFs), paired against low-yield DM debt
Valuation Tailwind: Brazil’s Equity Market Is Still Cheap
Brazil also has a deep and seasoned local investor base that has lived through multiple inflationary cycles. Decades of
inflation volatility have shaped investor behavior, making local institutions and individuals highly sensitive to rising inflation
and monetary tightening. This creates a persistent valuation discount in periods of high real rates—as is currently the
case.
However, that same sensitivity can create a powerful upside when disinflation takes hold. With inflation expected to fall
and the central bank signaling the end of its hiking cycle, a multi-year easing regime is likely. Historically, this transition
has acted as a catalyst for equity re-ratings. As real rates normalize and inflation fears recede, the valuation gap in
Brazilian equities could close rapidly, offering strong upside for both domestic and global investors.
Despite the structural improvements and macro tailwinds, Brazil’s equity market trades at a steep discount to both
developed and emerging market peers. As of mid-2025:
The Bovespa Index trades at a forward P/E of ~8.5x, compared to ~18x for the S&P 500 and ~12x for the MSCI
Emerging Markets index.
This valuation reflects years of underperformance during the software/tech-led cycle and macro skepticism but it
creates a powerful asymmetry now as Brazil enters a regime of capital inflows and commodity repricing.
With fiscal risk priced in, inflation moderating, and AI-era resource demand accelerating, mean reversion in Brazil’s
equity multiples could significantly boost returns even without earnings growth.
Strategic Non-Alignment: Why Both the U.S. and China Need Brazil
Brazil is emerging as a key “swing state” in the new global trade and infrastructure competition a geopolitically neutral,
resource-rich economy with deep ties to both China and the United States.

3

 China is Brazil’s largest trading partner, accounting for approximately 25–30% of Brazil’s total exports. Brazil runs a
substantial trade surplus with China, driven by commodity exports such as soybeans, iron ore, oil, and beef. China, in
turn, exports electronics, machinery, and fertilizers to Brazil. In 2024, Brazil’s trade surplus with China was estimated
between $50–60 billion.
The U.S. is Brazil’s second-largest trade partner, with a more balanced trade relationship. Brazil often runs a small trade
deficit with the U.S., importing chemicals, medical products, and industrial equipment, while exporting crude oil, aircraft,
steel, and agricultural goods.
This positioning creates a unique advantage:
China has invested heavily in Brazil’s lithium and EV infrastructure, including BYD’s plant and local mining rights.
The U.S., through the Export-Import Bank, is supporting Brazilian rare earth projects to counter China’s dominance in
critical minerals.
Brazil is not caught in the middle of a superpower conflict it is benefiting from both sides. Its neutral stance and commodity
leverage make it an attractive partner for friend-shoring, supply chain diversification, and long-term strategic investment
from both East and West.
Conclusion: Brazil Is the Misunderstood AI Infrastructure Trade
This is not an old-school EM inflation trade. It’s a modern macro regime shift trade. In a world of fiscal repression, AI
hardware bottlenecks, and green power scarcity, Brazil has what the next decade demands: materials, energy, stability,
and yield. The reward to risk opportunity for Brazil looks good not just as a trade but this is a secular turn from software to
hardware and a global trade restructuring in the first inning of a major shift in the world.

114276:
June 8, 2025

The Hidden Hand of AI: What’s Really Moving Markets, Jobs, and
Bitcoin in 2025
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

In this week’s video, I explore how artificial intelligence is accelerating structural shifts across markets, the labor force, and
geopolitics. While stock indices continue to rise and approach all-time highs, job growth is weakening beneath the
surface, particularly in sectors outside healthcare and leisure and travel. This divergence signals the emergence of a
“silent recession,” where AI-driven efficiency and cost-cutting are fueling corporate profits at the expense of labor demand.
This dynamic will continue to put pressure on the government to keep interest rates down and focus on liquidity and
growth rather than austerity, and will also impact the Fed in the coming months. I also highlight how embodied AI—robots,
drones, and autonomous systems—is moving us from a software-driven economy to one increasingly dependent on
hardware, semiconductors, electricity, and rare earth minerals.
The final part of the video connects AI to Bitcoin and stablecoins. I outline nine reasons why I think Bitcoin will surprise to
the upside this year, driven by expanding stablecoin volumes, institutional adoption, favorable political signals, and the
reawakening of innovation tokens such as Ethereum and Solana. I also highlight the significance of Circle’s IPO, one of
the largest underpricings in recent history, as a clear signal of how central stablecoins have become to the future of digital
finance. With the Genius Act still pending, 2025 is shaping up to be the year of stablecoins, driven by powerful network
effects as AI agents accelerate adoption. AI is becoming the bridge between the digital and traditional financial worlds,
enabling seamless transaction flows, automated decision-making, and scalable financial infrastructure. These
developments reinforce the idea that AI and crypto, two disruptive technologies, are no longer speculative narratives but
central forces shaping global capital markets in the new modern world.
Timestamps:
AI’s Expanding Influence Across Markets and Geopolitics (00:00–04:20)
AI is rapidly transforming the stock market, labor dynamics, and even global trade policy. The shift is from software to
hardware—driven by rising demand for semiconductors, power, and rare earths. I argue that AI has been central in
softening and now, in my opinion, ending the China US trade war, through the importance of the rare earth minerals in
embodied AI systems.
1. Broadening Market Strength and No Imminent Recession (00:40–01:21, 11:09–12:28)
Markets continue to rise, with small-cap indices like IWM showing bullish reversal patterns. Despite weakening job
data, there’s no evidence of a traditional, credit-driven recession. Companies are adapting with AI-driven productivity,
not widespread layoffs. Labor market softness is viewed as structural, not cyclical.
2. Misaligned Investor Sentiment and Strategy (03:03–03:21)
Investor sentiment remains overly bearish despite market strength. Data from Investors Intelligence shows that
sentiment has barely improved from 2022 levels. I highlight the difficulties for those still focused on outdated
frameworks like recession probabilities, arguing they are missing the AI-led market regime shift.
3. The Rise of Embodied AI and Energy Infrastructure Bottlenecks (04:23–06:40)
AI is entering the physical world—robots, drones, humanoids—requiring vast infrastructure upgrades. Mark
Andreessen highlights embodied AI as the next transformative wave, akin to his “software eats the world” thesis.
Securing energy and compute resources is becoming the central investment theme.

 4. Rare Earth Minerals and the New Trade Reality (18:07–24:00)
China’s export controls on rare earth elements have forced the U.S. back into trade talks. These minerals are essential
to defense, electronics, and AI hardware. Rubio and others now openly acknowledge U.S. vulnerability. Europe is also
scrambling to secure supply, including investments in Greenland. Rare earth control is shifting geopolitical leverage to
China.
5. Housing Market Risks Are Misinterpreted (15:23–16:46)
Although home affordability is strained due to high interest rates, delinquencies remain at record lows. Most U.S.
mortgages are locked in at low rates or paid off. I see the current housing fears as premature and argue rising rates will
eventually trigger liquidity; potentially driving renewed support for risk assets like Bitcoin.
6. AI-Driven Labor Market Restructuring (10:50–14:50)
Job growth in 2024 is concentrated in healthcare and government-supported sectors. Sectors like tech, mining, and
transportation show flat or negative hiring. The speaker describes a “silent recession,” where AI is reducing hiring
needs while maintaining revenue growth, transferring gains from labor to capital.
7. Bitcoin’s Setup for an Upside Breakout (35:12–40:16)
Bitcoin has been consolidating, but is poised for upside. Catalysts include stablecoin volume growth, institutional and
political support, and the return of utility tokens. The Genius Act and continued U.S. debt monetization are also cited as
tailwinds. I see parallels to the post-dot-com rebound, where innovation-led assets rebounded first.
8. The Lag in Utility and Innovation Tokens (35:44–37:20)
Most crypto tokens, especially Ethereum-related, remain near their lows from the 2022 crash. Despite weak price
action, technical setups suggest an eventual breakout. The speaker compares the environment to the early 2000s post
the Dot Com Bubble, when sentiment lagged the recovery. Breakouts in small caps and crypto may converge.
9. The Strategic Necessity of AI Literacy (29:01–31:36)
Daily use of AI is critical for professionals and students alike. I emphasize using tools like Perplexity, LLMs, and prompt
chaining for research, investing, and content creation. I reframe “hallucinations” as opportunities for creative
breakthroughs, similar to AlphaGo’s famous Move 37. Those who embrace AI will be best positioned to build future
wealth.
