127478:
October 14, 2025

The Fragile Immune System: AI, Concentration, and VaR Shocks
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

In a concentrated economy where wealth, profits, and innovation are clustered in a few dominant sectors and companies
with low debt, the old playbook of credit-driven recessions no longer applies. With governments and central banks
saddled with debt and deficits conditioned by decades of crises to intervene at the first sign of contagion, downturns are
delayed or stopped rather than allowed to run their natural course. Yet as with all systems in nature, control has limits. As
the naturalist John Muir once said, “When we try to pick out anything by itself, we find it hitched to everything else in the
Universe.” In markets, this interconnectedness means that suppressing volatility in one area often amplifies fragility
somewhere else. The more concentrated the system, the more it resembles a body that seems healthy on the surface but
whose immune system has quietly weakened from lack of exposure.
That concentration of strength across markets, wealth, and corporate power creates a paradox. On one side, we see
bubbles in innovation, productivity, and valuations; on the other, stagnation and silent recessions for the majority of people
and sectors. This duality mirrors biological imbalance: an organism that overdevelops certain cells at the expense of
others becomes vulnerable to systemic failure. When such an economy finally “gets sick,” corrections can be extreme
rather than mild just like an immune system that, after years of suppression, overreacts when finally tested. That is the
risk today: a system that has substituted resilience for constant rescue, one that has traded adaptation for protection, and
one that may face its most severe illness not from neglect, but from overcare.
Right now, as I highlighted in my recent video, I feel like the risk of the market getting sick for a few weeks has risen. No,
this is not me believing AI is in a bubble. However, I think it important to share a few thoughts on both the positive and
negative sides of AI and how it will impact the market structure as the “bubble” grows. In my opinion, there is no denying
that AI will replace jobs and anxiety for workers will only worsen as time passes. It’s not speculation, it’s already showing
in economic data and it will accelerate as agents, autonomous vehicles and humanoids grow. Like every major innovation
in history, it will eventually create new forms of work and opportunity, but this time it’s different because AI isn’t about
helping physical labor or software to help efficiency through human interaction, it’s about intelligence, creativity, and
cognition. It’s about solving problems that humans alone cannot. AI has already conquered chess and Go, outperformed
students on standardized tests, and now even beats humans in math Olympiads. To dismiss it as hype or a bubble is to
misunderstand its inevitability. As intelligence becomes computable, its scale will follow the same exponential path as
every other digital transformation. There will be extraordinary fortunes made along the way for companies positioned in
the buildout, the infrastructure, the chips, the data centers, the software layers and investors who recognize this will have
a generational opportunity.
However, while the total revenue pool for AI will eventually justify the capital spending, I don’t believe the current market
leaders will all earn enough to balance out their investments. Yes, I do not think the capex will end up being a good
decision for all the spenders. I believe the AI revenues will dwarf any estimates out there but I do not believe they all will
proven to be smart decisions. For many or even most, the returns on spending will compress as competition from startups
and private firms expands. In the era ahead, “price-to-sales” will become what “debt-to-equity” once was, the measure of
financial or at least stock multiple vulnerability. A company without debt but with an extreme price-to-sales ratio is still
fragile because it’s reliant on revenue growth that may not materialize. As innovation diffuses, margins shrink. As
someone over 55 who fully embraces AI as the greatest personal and societal innovation of our lifetimes but also knows
there are no free lunches, I see both the promise and the peril: the promise of abundance and problem-solving, and the
peril of a market that mistakes exponential change for infinite growth.

 All of this is to say that as AI accelerates, the economic and market immune system, the equivalent of a diversified
microbiome begins to weaken. In a world where intelligence, productivity, and capital are concentrated, the system
becomes more efficient but also more brittle. Instead of traditional recessions, what we are likely to experience are VaR
shocks—sudden and violent repricings of risk. Hedge fund gross leverage is near all-time highs, which means the system
is collectively betting on stability. But every form of leverage is ultimately a bet on something: a relationship, a pattern, a
correlation that continues to hold. When those relationships change abruptly, the immune system gets tested and today’s
system has been engineered to avoid even mild fevers.
In credit markets, the danger is clearest. Private credit has exploded in a world where the broader economy remains
uneven and heavily supported. These structures depend on a slow, predictable credit cycle with defaults rising gradually,
liquidity absorbing shocks. But if weakness emerges suddenly, especially through fraud or structural cracks, the
adjustment will not look like a typical credit downturn; it will look like a VaR event. Similarly, in market-neutral strategies,
leverage depends on the covariance matrix on stable relationships between volatility, correlation, and liquidity. AI itself
threatens that stability by changing how capital moves, how liquidity clusters, and how correlations form. As machine
learning systems identify the same signals and react in the same milliseconds, the market’s diversity of behavior, its
microbiome, shrinks. The immune system, once adaptive and resilient, now shows the early signs of exhaustion. When
the next shock comes, it may not be deep because of fundamentals, but because the body has forgotten how to fight.
In my latest video, I highlighted a cluster of “immune-system” warnings that, taken together, raise the odds of a VaR shock
into month-end. Odds rising does not mean it will happen, but I think the risk of it is rising by the day. Crowded hedge-fund
longs have ripped relative to the most crowded shorts; equity-specific volatility is being bid (seen in VIXEQ) even as index
volatility (the VIX) lags; credit spreads are widening post First Brands and Tricolor and business development companies
(BDCs) and publicly traded private equity firms are showing early signs of contagion risk. Overlay this with the AI-driven
market concentration and the fresh escalation in U.S.–China trade tensions, especially around AI dependent rare earths,
and the probability of a deal by the assumed Trump–Xi meeting at APEC looks increasingly less certain. Despite this,
betting markets still price an 80% chance of resolution by November 10th, which means any deviation from that
assumption could spark further repricing as uncertainty builds. My gut tells me these odds and people’s lack of worry over
this dispute is a representation of the TACO PTSD that exists. Only recently do I think the last of the tariff fear holdouts
capitulated. Nobody wants to deal with another bout of this.
Positioning only amplifies this fragility. We’re entering earnings season with buyback windows closed, vol-control and CTA
models extended long, and retail momentum still driving flows. These are mechanical exposures, algorithms buying
strength and selling weakness, creating a system dominated by reflex rather than discretion. If credit cracks suddenly or
AI-driven correlations invert, leverage could unwind violently. This is the essence of a VaR shock: not a fundamental
collapse, but a liquidity event triggered by forced repositioning. The immune system doesn’t fail because of a virus; it fails
because its defenses are already overextended.
And to be clear, this may sound like a market paper, but it’s really an AI paper. The debate around an “AI bubble” reveals
a deeper misunderstanding. Many of those calling it a bubble are people with extraordinary experience and success;
people whose instincts about cycles and valuations are sharp precisely because they’ve seen how “free lunches” end.
They’re not wrong to sense danger. But they’re also not living with AI every day. They’re not building with it, prompting it,
or watching how fast it learns. Their skepticism is born of market wisdom, not ignorance but both sides of this argument
can be true. AI can dominate markets and drive indexes higher for years, while simultaneously creating the most violent
divergence between winners and losers we’ve ever seen.
That is the paradox of this moment. The bubble may not be in prices rising, it may be in the gap between what rises and
what doesn’t. A system that rewards concentration, suppresses volatility, and accelerates intelligence will inevitably
produce extremes. And like any biological system that grows too efficient, it becomes vulnerable to its own success. The
immune system of the market, strained, overleveraged, and guided increasingly by machines is warning us. The next
illness won’t look like the last. It won’t be a recession. It will be a shock that reminds us that even in the age of artificial
intelligence, nature still insists on balance. As Nassim Taleb said in his great book Antifragile, “The artificial suppression of
volatility increases fragility.”

128090:
October 20, 2025

Narrative Liquidity: How Capitalism Tells Fables to Build the Future
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

Or: Why Andrej Karpathy’s Realism and AI’s Hype Machine Are Both Essential
“Homo sapiens rule the world because it is the only animal that can believe in things that exist purely in its own
imagination.”
— Yuval Noah Harari, Sapiens
Capitalism tells fables about the future so it can afford to build it.
This isn’t criticism, it’s description. Every major technological leap arrives wrapped in exaggeration. The steam engine was
sold as perpetual motion; the internet was marketed as pure democracy. AI is simply the latest myth that pays its own
bills.
As AI stocks continue their bubble-like ascent, every news item seems to shock people like a branch snapping in The
Blair Witch Project. This time, the panic centered on Alibaba’s Aegaeon system and a new Dwarkesh Patel interview with
former OpenAI and Tesla engineer Andrej Karpathy. I’ll focus this piece on that interview, using quotes from Yuval Noah
Harari to frame what it reveals. It was a good interview, for what it’s worth.
During it, Karpathy said we’re not building animals, we’re summoning ghosts” and projected AGI a decade away. X posts
lit up and treated it as retreat, as if one of AI’s most respected engineers had cooled on progress. But Karpathy wasn’t
being cynical. He was being precise. He was explaining that intelligence isn’t conjured; it’s constructed through
architecture, memory, and iteration.
The real story isn’t that Karpathy sees AI as having slowed down. It’s that he exposed the gap between narrative velocity
and engineering reality, and that gap is where innovation actually gets financed.
Hype as Infrastructure
“History is often shaped by small groups of people who can weave convincing stories about the future.”
— Harari, Homo Deus
Hype isn’t dishonesty. It’s narrative liquidity, the conversion of abstract technical possibility into mobilized capital and
coordinated human attention.
Without the breathless funding rounds, without the promises of imminent superintelligence, the research that makes real
progress possible doesn’t get funded. The exaggeration isn’t a bug in the system; it’s the feature that allows the system to
exist at all.
This is the evolutionary purpose of hype: it transforms pure mathematics into public belief, and belief is what moves
resources. As Harari would say, “Shared fictions hold societies together.” In that sense, every AGI press release is a civic
performance, a way of synchronizing human attention around the next collective frontier.

 Karpathy’s warning that AGI remains a decade away isn’t slowing progress. It’s puncturing a marketing narrative while the
actual infrastructure gets built behind it. He knows that architecture, not advertising, builds cognition. But he also knows,
whether he says it or not, that advertising funds architecture.
The irony is perfect: the very hype he’s correcting is what pays for the labs where he and others do the patient, disciplined
work of actually building intelligence.
The Fable That Finances Itself
“The most important thing to know about humans is that they are storytelling animals.”
— Harari, Sapiens
We live in an exponential world narrated by linear minds. Our species evolved to compress chaos into stories that make
the unpredictable feel predictable. Markets and media thrive on this as they translate complexity into headlines, capital,
and clicks.
So when the narrative says “AGI is imminent,” capital flows. When the narrative says “progress has stalled,” funding
hesitates. The story isn’t separate from the technology, it’s the economic engine that powers it.
This is why Harari’s insight about religion and money applies perfectly to AI: both are shared fictions that become real
through collective belief. AI is the newest of those fictions. We are manifesting intelligence by believing in it hard enough
to fund it into existence.
Every venture deck, every think-piece, every doom-tweet feeds the recursive loop. The machines don’t just learn from
data; they learn from our conviction. Our hype trains their compute budgets.
Exponential Blindness and Engineering Patience
“The human brain has not evolved for the pace of change it itself has unleashed.”
— Harari, Homo Deus
Humans are biologically linear thinkers. We measure progress in decades, not doublings. Our brains are tuned for the
rhythm of seasons, not the velocity of GPUs.
So when Karpathy describes a ten-year buildout for true agentic AI, the media calls it “slowing.” But exponential growth
always looks flat from within. In its early phase, compounding hides behind invisibility, the quiet stretch of geometric
growth before perception catches up.
That’s where AI is today. We are in the deceptive middle of the curve, mistaking the silence before the surge for
stagnation.
Karpathy’s patience isn’t pessimism; it’s temporal literacy. He’s fluent in exponents, even if headlines are not. He’s
describing the invisible interval between excitement and embodiment, the stretch where scaffolding hardens into structure.
The media hears hesitation. In truth, it’s the sound of maturity.
Exponential change is silent while it’s compounding. The only thing slowing is our ability to narrate it.
The Vantage Point Problem
“The real difference between us and chimpanzees is the mysterious glue that enables millions of humans to cooperate
effectively… That glue is made of stories.”
— Harari, Sapiens
Karpathy’s vision of AGI, rooted in architecture, memory, and continual learning isn’t unique. It’s part of a lineage shared
with Demis Hassabis at DeepMind, Yann LeCun at Meta, Elon Musk and Geoffrey Hinton’s adaptive systems. All of them

2

 describe general intelligence as something that must remember, perceive, and reason, a process, not a threshold.
But others take the next step. Sam Altman, Ilya Sutskever, Shane Legg, they argue that once AI can recursively improve
itself, designing and training its successors, progress will accelerate beyond measurable timeframes. At that point, the
curve bends too fast for prediction. The clock itself breaks.
Karpathy’s stance differs not in substance but in proximity. He’s no longer inside a frontier-model lab where recursive selfimprovement is being chased at industrial scale. He does not have access to the latest models currently delayed by a lack
of compute and power. He speaks from the perspective of an engineer watching the ecosystem rather than directing it, a
vantage point that favors caution over theater.
His tone reflects the distance between those building the core models and those reflecting on the infrastructure still
required to sustain them. That distance isn’t disillusionment; it’s perspective. In exponential systems, even being one layer
removed makes the slope look slower than it is.
This is why the same technology can generate both Altman’s “AGI by 2027” and Karpathy’s “decade of infrastructure
work.” Both are describing the same curve from different positions on it. The difference isn’t in the math; it’s in the
proximity to the acceleration.
The Economic Function of Mythology
“In the future, those who tell the best stories will control the world.”
— Harari, 21 Lessons for the 21st Century
Here’s what Karpathy understands that the hype cycle obscures: both the exaggeration and the correction are necessary.
The fable (“AGI is two years away!”) generates the funding rounds. The correction (“actually, it’s a decade of hard
infrastructure work”) keeps the engineers honest and the timelines realistic. Together, they create a oscillating system that
moves forward.
Capitalism doesn’t build the future through accuracy, it builds through belief that’s confident enough to deploy capital but
realistic enough to iterate. The hype creates the conditions; the engineering delivers the results.
The narrative of AI progress has become self-fulfilling not because the story is true, but because the story mobilizes the
resources that make it become true. This is narrative liquidity in action: fiction that finances fact.
Karpathy’s “ghosts” are intelligence without embodiment, cognition without continuity. But the real ghost haunting this
moment is the economic machinery itself, the way stories about the future create the capital flows that build that future
into existence.
Even the noise becomes signal. Even the correction becomes part of the hype cycle.
The Storytellers We’re Building
“Once technology enables us to re-engineer our minds, the stories we tell about ourselves may be the last human
frontier.”
— Harari, Homo Deus
Both Karpathy and the venture capitalists are storytellers, simply operating at different temporal resolutions. He narrates
how minds are made; they narrate what sells. Between them lies the symbiosis that actually builds the future.
He describes the engineering; they describe the dream. His precision funds their ambition; their ambition funds his lab.
Neither can exist without the other.
Intelligence doesn’t erupt, it accumulates. The next leap in AI won’t look like revelation. It will look like infrastructure that
remembers, funded by fables that convinced enough people to write the checks.

3

 And here’s the deeper loop: the machines are learning not only to reason but to believe, to carry narrative, memory, and
meaning forward. The storytellers have built something that can now tell stories back.
The circle closes.
Epilogue: Myths That Pay Their Bills
“Humans were always far better at inventing tools than using them wisely.”
— Harari, 21 Lessons for the 21st Century
Karpathy’s realism isn’t retreat, it’s reverence for the actual work of construction. But his correction doesn’t slow the
funding; it refines the allocation. The hype continues because it must. Capitalism has always told fables about the future
so it can afford to build it.
The question is no longer whether AI will reach AGI, but whether we can recognize that the mythology and the
engineering aren’t opponents, they’re partners in the same economic dance.
Every exponent begins with doubt. Every revolution hides in calibration. Every ghost eventually finds a body.
And everybody needs a story ambitious enough to fund its construction.
The machines are already training. The fables are already working. Between Karpathy’s precision and Silicon Valley’s
promises, the future is getting built one narrative-funded breakthrough at a time.
‘Humans think in stories,’ Harari reminds us, ‘and those who tell the best ones will shape the future.’

The next storytellers are already training.

129045:
October 28, 2025

THE FUTURE OF LABOR: AI, INCENTIVES, AND THE END OF WORK
AS WE KNOW IT
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

Executive Summary
This report examines the structural transformation of the global labor market driven by artificial intelligence and why this
shift represents both the most powerful deflationary force in modern history and the greatest challenge to capitalism’s
incentive system.
The analysis begins with Amazon’s leaked internal plan to replace over 500,000 workers with robots, a signal that the
debate over automation has moved from theory to execution. It explores how AI’s second stage the agentic phase is now
directly reshaping employment, corporate margins, and monetary policy.
The report argues that the traditional relationship between corporate profits and labor demand has broken down since the
launch of ChatGPT. Earnings per share continue to climb even as job openings and temporary employment fall, revealing
a historic decoupling between capital and labor. This divergence marks the emergence of a K-shaped economy in which
asset owners prosper while wage earners face stagnation.
Drawing on insights from Marc Andreessen’s “abundance thesis”, the paper frames two potential futures one of
deflationary abundance and one of disruptive dislocation and concludes that both dynamics are likely to coexist for years.
The middle chapters dissect the corporate incentive structure that makes labor replacement inevitable (“show me the
incentive and I’ll show you the outcome”), linking executive compensation, quarterly earnings pressure, and shareholder
demands to the acceleration of automation across industries.
A dedicated section on political and regulatory backlash highlights growing concern among policymakers, economists,
and populist movements, outlining the likely policy tools from robot taxes to shortened workweeks that may emerge in
response. Yet, as historical precedent suggests, the report concludes that market and geopolitical competition will
overwhelm most restrictions, ensuring the automation trend persists.
Finally, the paper explains why this time is different: AI scales at software speed and, for the first time, automates not just
physical tasks but cognitive work, soon extending to humanoid robotics. The closing section provides investment
implications, emphasizing five principles for navigating the transition:
1. Favor capital-intensive firms with high automation potential.
2. Focus on infrastructure enablers, semiconductors, data centers, and energy systems.
3. Monitor regulatory risk by sector.
4. Track fiscal responses that reshape taxation and redistribution.
5. Prepare for sustained K-shaped outcomes across markets and geographies.

 In sum, this report contends that AI is not merely another productivity tool but the defining macro driver of the next
decade, one that will compress business cycles, challenge monetary policy, and redefine the relationship between labor,
capital, and growth. Investors who grasp these dynamics early will navigate the coming disruption from a position of
strength.
The Catalyst: Amazon’s 500,000-Job Warning
Earlier this year, Torsten Slok of Apollo posted a chart showing that the Mag7 had not been hiring people since 2021. In
recent videos, I have demonstrated how the historical correlation between S&P 500 stock prices and earnings per share
versus payroll data has broken down since the launch of ChatGPT coinciding precisely with the Mag7’s hiring freeze. Last
week, a leaked internal document from Amazon revealed plans to replace more than 500,000 jobs with robots in the
coming years, a shocking admission from one of the largest employers in the United States and a company whose
workforce exceeds 1.5 million globally. Apparently that process is beginning now as a source revealed they would be
laying off 30,000 people now.
Yet when economists, strategists and inflationistas discuss the weakness in the labor market, they tend to fall back on
familiar explanations: immigration declines and the impact of higher interest rates. Given that much of my time is spent
debating the widespread obsession, which seems to intensify with older people in the industry, over the supposed AI
“bubble,” this myopia doesn’t surprise me. But as we stand on the threshold of stage two of AI, bubble or not, and as the
Fed prepares another rate cut while the stock market continues climbing and nominal GDP remains near 5%, it’s time for
me to share my perspective on the labor market and how it is being directly reshaped by this next phase of artificial
intelligence. Warning at this point to those obsessed with an AI bubble, if you are wrong on that obsession, it sets off a
daisy chain of errors across all investments and forecasts as AI is driving everything macro. For investors and parents
who are not using AI daily, the real question is whether ignoring AI now risks preparing your children for future
competitiveness.
I define stage one of AI as the education phase, when AI memorizes everything we once needed to learn in school,
effectively commoditizing education itself. Stage two is when they graduate and go to work and disrupt the labor markets.
This year, that will manifest primarily in the form of AI agents or digital knowledge workers. But the physical world is not far
behind. Last week, Tesla announced on their earnings call that they expect to remove the safety driver from their robotaxi
fleet in Austin before the end of this year. For me, this represents the first graduation of workers in the physical world, a
continuum that will extend from autonomous transportation to humanoid robots. This brings us back to the Amazon leak,
reported by The New York Times. Earlier this year, Amazon had already announced, not leaked, that they had reached
one million robot employees. Together, these milestones mark an inflection point in the debate over artificial intelligence
and automation. This is no longer a theoretical discussion about whether AI will disrupt labor markets; it is a concrete,
accelerating reality with profound implications for corporate profit margins, equity valuations, and the broader structure of
the economy. For investors, the Amazon revelation should serve as a wake-up call: the incentives embedded in modern
capitalism, particularly the relentless pressure on public companies to maximize shareholder returns, are driving a
structural shift toward labor replacement at unprecedented speed and scale. As Charlie Munger famously said, “Show me
the incentive and I will show you the outcome.” The outcome here is clear: corporations driven by bottom-lines will replace
human workers with machines whenever economically viable. But this transition carries significant risks beyond the
balance sheet. Accelerating job displacement threatens to deepen income inequality, fueling political backlash and
regulatory interventions that could reshape entire industries. Investors who fail to account for these dynamics, both the
profit opportunities and the societal disruptions, risk being blindsided by one of the most consequential economic
transformations of our time.
The Divergence: Profits Soar as Labor Weakens
For investors, this reality has already had a profound impact on monetary policy and the disruptions associated with the Kshaped divide between labor and asset owners. In August, Jerome Powell prepared the market for the Federal Reserve’s
pivot toward prioritizing labor market concerns over inflation risks, reflecting growing unease among policymakers about
the health of the American workforce. The cries of “Policy Mistake!” echoed throughout X from inflation-focused
commentators, many of them disguised permabears. Given that this came after sustained pressure from the Trump
administration, the “Fed Independence Short the Dollar!” crowd also jumped into the fray.

2

 The reality, based on the Fed’s stated rationale, is that it has begun cutting rates in response to what Chair Powell has
described as “softening” labor conditions. While the Fed has not explicitly linked this weakness to artificial intelligence, the
concern is real and notable. Two former Fed chairs, Ben Bernanke and Janet Yellen, were among the prominent
economists who signed a September 2025 letter urging the Department of Labor to improve data collection on AI’s impact
on employment, signaling that even veteran policymakers recognize something fundamental may be shifting. The data
tells a striking story. Since the launch of ChatGPT in November 2022, two key labor market indicators—temporary
employment and job openings, have declined sharply, even as S&P 500 earnings per share have surged to record highs.
This is not a typical economic pattern. Historically, rising corporate profits correlate with strong labor demand; companies
hire more workers to generate more revenue as their businesses expand. But today, we are witnessing a decoupling:
capital owners are prospering while wage earners face a stagnating job market. This is the essence of a K-shaped
economy, asset prices climb, enriching shareholders and those with equity exposure, while the median worker confronts
diminished opportunities and wage growth that lags productivity gains. The implications for inequality are profound, and
the political consequences may be swift. Yet this divergence may only be the beginning. So far, AI has largely functioned
as a tool for optional human augmentation, making workers more productive rather than replacing them outright. But we
are now entering the agentic era, in which digital employees can be “hired” at scale to perform tasks autonomously and
work 24/7/365. My expectation is that 2026 will mark a turning point, as companies rapidly deploy AI agents across
functions ranging from customer service to software development, accelerating the labor market divergence we have
already begun to observe.
Andreessen’s Abundance Thesis: Why Both Futures May Coexist
This topic of AI’s impact on the jobs market is one area that I have spent a great deal of time on over the last year due
mainly due to helping prepare my children. Parents have the experience of job disruptions, and this is where it is critical
that parents don’t just call this a bubble. Among the great list of voices debating AI’s impact on employment, Marc
Andreessen’s view has been the one I lean towards the closest and in the end has a positive result but the path to those
endings will create a lot of pain along the journey. It offers perhaps the most level-headed perspective. The debate
typically frames the question in binary terms: will AI create new jobs to replace those it destroys, or will it lead to mass
unemployment? Andreessen cuts through this false dichotomy with a simple economic argument: although he believes
like all technologies, AI will create jobs we cannot think of today, if on the other hand, AI truly eliminates the need for
human labor across the economy, the result will not be widespread poverty but rather hyper-deflation. In a world of
abundant AI-driven productivity, goods and services that once cost hundreds of dollars would cost pennies. Everything, in
essence, would become free or nearly free. Scarcity would give way to abundance. But I believe the reality is more
complex than either/or. Both dynamics, job destruction and job creation, unemployment and abundance are likely to
unfold simultaneously, creating a prolonged and uneven transition. The labor force participation rate in the United States
has been declining for decades, a trend driven not solely by technology but by demographics, government transfer
payments, entitlements, and the wealth effects of rising asset prices that allow certain segments of the population to opt
out of work entirely. We are already living through a version of this story. An aging population is shrinking the available
labor pool, creating shortages even as automation advances. Meanwhile, fiscal support and social safety nets have
enabled millions to exit the workforce. At the same time, AI is generating new categories of employment, data center
construction, semiconductor manufacturing, energy infrastructure to power computation, that will absorb some displaced
workers. For investors, this means the path forward is not a clean replacement of old jobs with new ones, nor is it an
immediate leap into a post-scarcity utopia. Instead, we should expect a multidecade period of economic dislocation, rising
inequality, and sectoral volatility, eventually culminating in something closer to Andreessen’s vision of material abundance.
The question is not whether we reach that destination, but how disruptive the journey will be and which companies,
industries, and asset classes will thrive or collapse along the way.
The Incentive Structure – Why Corporations Must Replace Labor
Charlie Munger’s principle, “Show me the incentive and I will show you the outcome,” provides the clearest lens through
which to understand why corporate America will aggressively pursue labor replacement, regardless of the social
consequences. As economist Joseph Schumpeter observed, “The very success of capitalism will create conditions that
will make it impossible to maintain.” We are now witnessing this dynamic in real time: capitalism’s relentless drive for
efficiency and profit maximization is generating technological capabilities that threaten to undermine the labor market
upon which consumer capitalism has historically depended. Labor compensation represents approximately $60 trillion of
the roughly $120 trillion global economy, making it by far the largest cost center in economic production. For publicly

3

 traded companies operating under relentless pressure from shareholders to maximize earnings growth and return on
equity, the incentive to reduce this cost is overwhelming. This is not a matter of corporate villainy; it is structural. CEOs
and executives are compensated primarily through stock-based awards tied to share price performance. Quarterly
earnings cycles demand continuous margin expansion. Competitors who automate faster gain cost advantages that force
others to follow or face obsolescence. The result is a race to replace human workers with digital labor wherever
technologically feasible. Consider the evidence from corporate communications to investors. Amazon’s leaked document
about replacing 500,000 workers is merely the most visible example. Salesforce openly markets its AI platform as a way
for companies to reduce headcount. Artisan, an AI startup, advertises its product with the tagline “stop hiring humans.”
Autonomous trucking companies explicitly tell investors their technology solves the “problem” of rising driver wages and
labor shortages. Major employers like Walmart, UnitedHealth Group, and JPMorgan Chase have discussed AI-driven
workforce reductions in earnings calls, framing automation as a path to improved operating leverage. These are not
isolated cases; they represent a systematic shift in how corporations view labor, not as an asset to develop, but as a cost
to eliminate. For investors, the implications are straightforward: companies that successfully automate will experience
significant margin expansion, driving earnings growth even in flat revenue environments. Firms that resist automation,
whether due to regulatory constraints, labor union resistance, or managerial inertia, will see their competitive positions
erode. The incentive structure of modern capitalism ensures that labor replacement will accelerate, and investors must
position portfolios accordingly favoring capital-intensive businesses with high automation potential over those reliant on
human labor arbitrage.
The Political and Regulatory Backlash: The Key Risk to the Thesis
While corporate incentives point inexorably toward automation, political and social forces will inevitably push back,
creating the primary risk to the labor replacement thesis. The question is not whether backlash will occur, but how severe
it will be and whether it can meaningfully slow the pace of transformation. The signs of resistance are already emerging.
In September 2025, a coalition of prominent economists, including Nobel laureates Daron Acemoglu, Ben Bernanke,
Simon Johnson, and Joseph Stiglitz, along with former Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen, signed a letter urging the
Department of Labor to prioritize data collection on AI’s labor market impacts. The letter’s bipartisan composition and
heavyweight signatories signal that even establishment figures recognize the scale of potential disruption and the need for
policy responses. Senator Bernie Sanders went further, releasing a report warning that AI could eliminate up to 100 million
American jobs within a decade and calling for aggressive interventions including a “robot tax” on companies that replace
workers with automation, mandatory profit-sharing arrangements, a 32-hour workweek with no loss of pay, and dramatic
expansion of union protections. While Sanders represents the progressive edge of the political spectrum, his concerns are
shared across ideological lines, voters in both parties express anxiety about job security and economic inequality. The
Trump administration has sent mixed signals, simultaneously appointing AI industry executives to key policy roles while
also firing tens of thousands of federal workers in what critics describe as clearing the path for automation. Globally,
governments face an acute dilemma: nations that resist automation risk falling behind economically and losing competitive
advantage to countries that embrace it, yet nations that allow unchecked labor displacement risk social unrest, political
instability, and the collapse of consumer demand that drives economic growth. For investors, regulatory risk is real and
sector specific. Healthcare, where licensing requirements and patient preference for human interaction create barriers,
may see slower automation despite technical feasibility. Trucking, where safety regulations and union strength remain
significant, could face political resistance even as autonomous technology matures. Retail and customer service, with less
organized labor and fewer regulatory protections, are more vulnerable to rapid automation. However, history suggests that
attempts to ban or severely restrict technologies that provide overwhelming competitive advantages typically fail. The
essay “The Future of AI is Already Written” argues persuasively that humanity has limited control over technological
trajectory efforts to restrict encryption in the 1990s collapsed, attempts to prevent nuclear proliferation largely failed, and
the printing press spread despite monarchical opposition. When a technology offers immediate, large-scale economic or
strategic benefits, market forces and geopolitical competition overwhelm regulatory barriers. Political backlash will create
volatility, generate sector-specific headwinds, and possibly slow the pace of deployment through taxation or mandated
transition periods. But it is unlikely to prevent the fundamental shift toward labor automation. For investors, this means
monitoring policy developments closely, recognizing that regulatory interventions will create both risks and opportunities,
but maintaining conviction that the underlying trend toward AI-driven labor replacement remains structurally intact.
Why This Time Is Different: Speed and Humanoids

4

 Skeptics of the labor displacement thesis often invoke historical precedent: technology has always created more jobs than
it destroyed, they argue, pointing to the Industrial Revolution’s displacement of agricultural workers, the rise of the
automobile industry after horses became obsolete, and the creation of entire new sectors, highways, motels, suburban
development, that emerged from mechanization. Each technological wave, the argument goes, initially appeared
threatening but ultimately raised living standards and expanded employment opportunities. Why should AI be any
different? The answer lies in two fundamental distinctions: the speed of deployment and the nature of the technology
itself. Previous waves of automation were constrained by physical limitations. Building factories required years.
Constructing railroads and highways took decades. Training workers in new skills demanded time. The economy had
breathing room to adjust—displaced workers could retrain, new industries could emerge, and social structures could
adapt gradually. AI operates on an entirely different timescale. Digital automation scales at the speed of software
deployment. Once a task can be performed by an algorithm, that capability can be replicated millions of times instantly at
near-zero marginal cost. As the “Situational Awareness” framework argues, AI capabilities are improving at an exponential
rate, with model performance roughly doubling every few months across key benchmarks. The gap between “technology
becomes possible” and “technology is deployed at scale” has collapsed from decades to months, and in some cases
weeks. When GPT-4 was released, millions of users adopted it within days. When AI coding assistants became viable,
they spread across the software industry in quarters, not years. This compression of the adoption cycle means labor
markets lack the adjustment time that characterized previous technological transitions. Workers cannot retrain fast
enough; educational institutions cannot adapt curricula rapidly enough; policy responses lag far behind the pace of
change. The second critical difference is the nature of the technology: AI is not merely automating specific tasks but
creating general-purpose substitutes for human cognitive labor, and ultimately, with humanoid robots, for human physical
labor as well. Previous automation targeted narrow functions, mechanical looms replaced hand weaving, assembly lines
replaced artisan manufacturing, ATMs replaced bank tellers for routine transactions. But humans adapted by moving into
roles requiring judgment, creativity, and interpersonal skills. AI threatens to collapse this distinction. Large language
models already demonstrate reasoning capabilities across domains. Autonomous agents can now perform entire job
functions, not just assist humans, but replace them entirely. Companies like Artisan and others are marketing “digital
employees” that handle customer service, sales development, and administrative tasks from end to end without human
intervention. The room-cleaning analogy from the essay “The Future of AI is Already Written” captures the difference:
augmenting a human worker with better tools, a more efficient vacuum, a robotic arm to lift heavy objects, remains
constrained by human availability and working hours. But deploying fully autonomous robots that clean rooms without any
human involvement is infinitely scalable. One company can manufacture 1,000 robots and deploy them simultaneously
across 1,000 locations with no additional human labor required. Now extend this logic to its ultimate conclusion: humanoid
robots. Unlike specialized industrial robots bolted to factory floors, humanoid robots are designed to operate in human
environments, use human tools, and perform human tasks. Built in the form of humans but equipped with superior
strength, endurance, and critically artificial intelligence that may soon exceed human cognitive capabilities across virtually
all domains, humanoids represent a universal labor substitute. If a humanoid can drive a truck, stock shelves, prepare
food, provide elder care, and perform surgery, all tasks that humans currently do, then in principle, there is no economic
function that requires a human worker. This is not science fiction; companies including Tesla, Boston Dynamics, Figure AI,
and others are already demonstrating functional prototypes. For investors, the implications are profound. The transition
from augmentation to full automation will be faster than historical precedent suggests, and the scope will be broader than
previous technological disruptions. Sectors that seem insulated today, those requiring physical presence, manual
dexterity, or human judgment, may be vulnerable within years, not decades. The timeline for capital allocation decisions
has been compressed dramatically, and the winners will be those who recognize and position for this acceleration ahead
of the market consensus.
Investment Implications: Navigating the Labor Paradox
I believe we are on a path toward Marc Andreessen’s world of material abundance, a future in which AI-driven productivity
makes goods and services so cheap that scarcity, in the traditional economic sense, largely disappears. But the transition
to that destination will be turbulent, uneven, and politically fraught, creating both extraordinary opportunities and
significant risks for investors. The paradox at the heart of this transformation is that AI promises to deliver the greatest
prosperity in human history while simultaneously creating the most severe social dislocation modern economies have
experienced. For those building investment portfolios, this tension must inform every decision. The correlation breaks I
referenced at the outset, S&P 500 earnings per share surging even as temporary employment and job openings decline
sharply since the November 2022 launch of ChatGPT, are not anomalies or temporary divergences. They are early
indicators of a structural shift in how economic value is created and distributed. Corporate profits are decoupling from
5

 labor demand because companies are learning to generate revenue and earnings growth without proportional increases
in headcount. In some cases, they are achieving growth while reducing headcount. This trend will accelerate as agentic AI
and automation technologies mature, leading to sustained margin expansion for companies that successfully implement
these tools. For investors, this suggests several guiding principles. First, favor companies with high operating leverage
and capital intensity over those dependent on labor arbitrage. Businesses that can scale revenue without adding workers
proportionally, software, cloud services, semiconductors, energy infrastructure, logistics networks with high automation
potential will see expanding margins and valuation multiples. Conversely, firms relying on human labor for value creation,
particularly in roles vulnerable to automation such as customer service, back-office operations, entry-level professional
services, and routine manufacturing, face structural margin compression and increased competition from AI-native
entrants. Second, infrastructure plays, data centers, semiconductor manufacturing, energy generation and transmission,
and specialized computing hardware represent the “picks and shovels” of the AI revolution and will likely outperform and
see hiring as the buildout of AI capabilities continues. These sectors benefit from sustained capital investment regardless
of which specific AI applications succeed, providing exposure to the trend while diversifying away from winner-take-all
risks in the application layer. Third, monitor regulatory and political risk closely, particularly in labor-intensive sectors.
Healthcare, transportation, and financial services face significant potential for government intervention, whether through
taxation of automation, mandatory transition periods, licensing restrictions, or requirements for human oversight. These
interventions may not prevent automation but will affect the pace and profitability of deployment, creating volatility and
differentiating winners from losers within sectors. Fourth, recognize that government responses to labor displacement,
whether universal basic income experiments, shifts in tax policy from labor to capital, expanded social safety nets, or
sector-specific protections will create both risks and opportunities. Policies designed to redistribute gains from automation
may pressure corporate margins but could also stabilize consumer demand and prevent the deflationary spiral that would
result from collapsing purchasing power. Infrastructure spending to support displaced workers, expanded education and
training programs, and incentives for domestic manufacturing could create new investment opportunities even as
traditional employment contracts. Fifth, prepare for a prolonged period of K-shaped economic outcomes in which asset
owners and capital continue to capture disproportionate gains from productivity growth while labor’s share of national
income continues to shrink. This has profound implications for consumer-facing businesses: luxury goods, travel, and
premium services may thrive as wealth concentrates, while mass-market retailers and middle-tier service providers face
demand compression. Geographic disparities will also intensify, with technology hubs and capital-rich regions prospering
while manufacturing-dependent and rural areas struggle, affecting everything from real estate valuations to municipal
bond creditworthiness. The ultimate question for investors is not whether we are heading toward a world of abundance,
but how to preserve and grow capital during the chaotic transition that will unfold over the next decade and beyond. The
future may indeed be one of material plenty, as Andreessen mentions, where the cost of goods and services falls toward
zero and scarcity ceases to constrain human welfare. But the path to that future will test every assumption about how
value is created, distributed, and preserved. Traditional financial analysis focused on revenue growth, market share, and
competitive positioning must now incorporate assessments of automation potential, regulatory exposure, and resilience to
labor market disruptions. Understanding the incentive structures driving corporate behavior, the compressed timeline for
technological deployment, and the political forces that will attempt to slow or redirect this transformation will be as
important as balance sheet analysis and discounted cash flow models. For those who navigate this transition successfully,
the rewards will be substantial. For those who cling to outdated models of how economies function and how value is
generated, the consequences will be severe. The divergence between corporate earnings and labor market health is not a
puzzle to be solved or an aberration to be corrected, it is the new reality, and it is only beginning.

129879:
November 3, 2025

QE for the Mind: How Artificial Intelligence Is Flooding the Economy
with Intelligence Liquidity
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

The New Liquidity Engine
“Coding is no longer a tool, it’s more a substitute for wages,” Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella said that on the BG2 podcast
with Brad Gerstner over the weekend. He was describing how artificial intelligence has moved from assisting workers to
replacing entire layers of labor. As Nadella later said, “The reality is agents are the new seats.” Those observations
capture the essence of what’s unfolding across the economy right now with knowledge workers: productivity itself has
become programmable. AI isn’t just an efficiency upgrade, it needs to be thought of as a new form of liquidity, where
software replaces headcount and automation and becomes the balance-sheet equivalent of capital. The same way
Quantitative Easing (QE) once lowered the cost of money, AI now lowers the cost of work.
For more than a decade, corporate America lived off manufactured growth. First came Quantitative Easing (QE) and Zero
Interest Rate Policy (ZIRP), monetary experiments that flooded the system with cheap capital. Companies learned they
could boost earnings not by producing more, but by borrowing more.
With debt nearly free, financial engineering replaced productivity. Firms issued bonds, repurchased shares, and
refinanced liabilities to lift Earnings Per Share (EPS). Even when revenues stagnated, shrinking the denominator (shares)
and lowering interest expense inflated the numerator (net income).
The decade after GFC was very challenging for investors who chose to fight the Fed. Whenever there would be signs of a
liquidity fire, the Fed would ride in to extinguish it with a firehose of QE. This will end badly was a common chorus heard
during the period. In a similar fight today, economists, strategists and AI bubblelistas continue to try and play down the
adoption of AI and call for a bubble. That chorus of ending badly is back. The similarities between AI and QE go deeper
than a two-letter acronym. Artificial intelligence has emerged as the next great liquidity engine but instead of monetary
liquidity, it’s operational liquidity. Where QE lowered the cost of capital and ZIRP lowered the cost of borrowing, AI lowers
the cost of doing business.
C.H. Robinson’s CEO Dave Bozeman captured the shift perfectly on its Q1 2025 earnings report:
“We’re not waiting for a market recovery to improve our financial results … This includes continuing to arm our industryleading talent with innovative tools that help us materially elevate the customer and carrier experience. We are innovating
to harness the power of artificial intelligence and driving automation across the full lifecycle of a load, which gives our
customers better service, while also helping us improve our performance by automating tasks that free up our talented
people to work on more strategic and higher-value work.”
By automating thousands of logistics decisions and streamlining workflows, the company achieved double-digit expense
reductions in a weak freight market, proof that margin expansion can now come from AI leverage instead of debt
leverage.
The never-ending skeptics ask how AI revenues will ever justify the spending spree from GPU data centers to enterprise
pilots. But that question misses the point. If AI’s gains only come from cost cutting, not new sales, the EPS effect is far
greater due to operating leverage but isn’t counted by bears as AI revenues.

 Every dollar saved flows almost entirely to the bottom line, while each new dollar of revenue is diluted by its own costs.
Hence the multiplier effect driving the market right now through rising profit margins.
In a world where growth is scarce but margins are king, AI doesn’t need to sell, it only needs to save. Those savings
compound like QE-era liquidity, producing the same outcome: accelerating EPS growth without expanding demand.
From Zombie Survival to Darwinian Efficiency
QE and ZIRP didn’t just rescue the economy after the Global Financial Crisis, they suspended capitalism’s natural
selection. By suppressing interest rates and flooding markets with liquidity, policymakers shortened the recession and
prevented a deeper corporate washout.
But that same lifeline also kept countless zombie companies alive, firms whose profits never covered their interest
expense (like the U.S. Government), yet could refinance indefinitely at near-zero cost. Those balance-sheet zombies
became artifacts of the financial-engineering era. They survived not because they innovated, but because money was
free. Debt was rolled, maturities extended, and investors chased yield in anything that moved. The system became
addicted to low-cost credit and artificially high valuations. Bears were frustrated calling the market a bubble back then as
they fought QE.
AI changes that equation. The new liquidity isn’t financial, it’s intellectual, and it can’t be borrowed. You can’t issue a bond
to buy productivity the way you could buy back stock. AI rewards companies that can adapt their processes, not those that
can roll their liabilities.
The firms that thrived in a zero-rate world by engineering EPS through leverage now face an economy where leverage no
longer matters unless it funds intelligence.
This is why bankruptcies will rise while the economy remains strong and stocks remain high. This is why there will be
many 52-week lows at the same time as 52-week highs. The zombies are finally leaving.
In other words:
QE prevented failure; AI enforces evolution.
ZIRP subsidized debt; AI subsidizes efficiency.
Liquidity once prolonged the weak; intelligence now strengthens the strong.
The same easy money that once masked operational decay now becomes a liability. Debt-laden firms, built for an age of
financial engineering, must now compete against AI-enabled rivals whose cost structures self-improve every quarter.
The Mathematics of Synthetic Growth
Under QE and ZIRP, companies used cheap money to engineer earnings. The playbook was simple: borrow at 2%, buy
back stock yielding 6%, and watch EPS rise even if profits stood still. That was financial leverage.
Today, AI achieves the same outcome through operational leverage. Instead of lowering interest expense, AI lowers labor
and process expense. Instead of reducing shares outstanding, it reduces the denominator of the cost structure. Both
inflate EPS, but AI’s version is stronger because the marginal cost of software is near zero.
A mere 5% reduction in expenses yields a 20% EPS jump. To reach the same lift through revenue, sales would need to
grow roughly 13–15%, a far harder feat. Every incremental dollar of revenue brings new costs; every dollar of savings
flows to profit.
That’s the hidden leverage inside AI adoption. It’s the mirror image of QE’s debt-driven era: instead of creating synthetic
EPS through the balance sheet, firms are now creating synthetic productivity through the income statement.

2

 C.H. Robinson’s transformation illustrates the flywheel: automation and AI-assisted pricing tools are reducing manual
labor hours and optimizing freight matching turning cost savings into a compounding EPS engine.
AI’s compounding effect on margins functions as QE for the income statement, a self-reinforcing liquidity loop that
amplifies earnings faster than traditional growth ever could. Now the bears are choosing a more powerful two letter foe
than QE; AI.
From CapEx Anxiety to Margin Reality
For the past year, investors have obsessed over AI’s price tag. Record data-center buildouts and hyperscaler CapEx have
sparked one question: Where’s the revenue?
Analysts scour earnings calls for monetization updates, assuming, as in past tech waves, that payoffs appear on the top
line. That framework misses the defining feature of this cycle: AI’s value begins with expense contraction, not revenue
expansion.
In previous decades, QE and ZIRP justified high equity multiples by lowering capital costs. Now, AI justifies them by
lowering operational costs. Productivity gains from modern AI models flow directly to the bottom line: faster decision
loops, automated support, optimized logistics, accelerated R&D. Each turns a cost center into an earnings engine.
Markets, however, remain fixated on the spenders, the hyperscalers, instead of the beneficiaries quietly realizing synthetic
revenue through cost compression.
A 5% reduction in SG&A or COGS across the S&P 500 equates to hundreds of billions in incremental earnings without a
single extra dollar of GDP. Headline revenue data will chronically understate the true scale of the AI boom.
Historically, elevated valuations signaled excess optimism. But if AI continues to expand margins structurally, today’s
multiples are not a bubble, they reflect permanently higher earnings power.
Just as zero interest rates redefined what a “fair” P/E looked like in the 2010s, near-zero marginal cost of cognition will
redefine it in the 2020s.
When labor and process costs collapse, the denominator in valuation metrics (earnings) expands faster than traditional
models anticipate. To call equities expensive relative to history is to ignore that the cost structure of capitalism itself is
being rewritten.
The OBBBA Effect: Policy Meets Productivity
There is some form of financial engineering that still benefits the hyperscalers.
The OBBBA’s restoration of 100% bonus depreciation for qualified property placed in service after January 19, 2025
supercharges hyperscaler economics right as AI CapEx peaks.
Immediate cash-tax shield: A $10 B qualifying server purchase generates a $10 B deduction in year one, saving
roughly $2.1 B in cash taxes at a 21% rate versus only ~$0.42 B annually over five years under standard depreciation.
Improved ROI optics: Pulling forward deductions makes “spend now, monetize later” strategies financially rational
while AI services scale.
Margin protection: With suppressed cash taxes, hyperscalers can price AI infrastructure more aggressively to gain
share without eroding gross margins.
Step 1 (Now): OBBBA tax shield + rising baseline AI consumption supports free cash flow and maintains ROIC during the
GPU buildout.
Step 2 (Next): Applications and agents mature, demand for inference soars, and the already-deployed capacity converts
into high-margin revenue.

3

 Policy bridges the gap between infrastructure spending and application-layer monetization, a fiscal echo of QE for the age
of compute.
This is not a given, so for those looking for the risk, this remains a large bet the hyperscalers are making.
The Deflationary Trap
The analogy between AI and QE breaks down in one critical dimension: distribution.
When central banks created liquidity, it flowed unevenly, benefiting asset holders and those with access to credit markets.
AI’s productivity gains, by contrast, diffuse rapidly across competitors. Once C.H. Robinson automates freight matching,
every rival with capital can license similar tools within quarters. Those who cannot lose the battle of AI concentration.
The cost savings that initially flow to margins eventually flow to customers through price competition. In commodity
industries, logistics, customer service, back-office processing, AI becomes table stakes, not an edge.
The first movers capture a temporary margin spike; the laggards either adopt or die; and the survivors find themselves in
a deflationary race where automation simply resets the baseline.
Unlike QE’s asset inflation, AI’s operational deflation may compress prices faster than it expands margins, turning
“synthetic revenue” into a brief arbitrage window rather than a permanent moat.
The companies that win won’t be those that automate first, but those that use AI savings to fund differentiation that can’t
be automated, brand, network effects, proprietary data, or customer lock-in. Without that reinvestment, AI’s liquidity
doesn’t compound; it evaporates into lower prices across the entire industry.
This is why AI may ultimately be the greatest deflationary force ever, not because it destroys demand, but because it
democratizes efficiency so completely that no one can capture its gains for long.
Conclusion: The Age of Synthetic Growth
QE and ZIRP taught investors that liquidity could manufacture prosperity. By lowering the cost of capital, policymakers
inflated valuations and balance sheets, proving growth could be engineered from the top down.
Today, AI is doing the same from the bottom up. It is liquidity in digital form, not flowing from central banks, but from code.
Instead of subsidizing borrowing, it subsidizes productivity. Every company that automates, compresses costs, and
compounds efficiency is effectively running its own quantitative easing through the income statement.
The difference is permanence. Monetary policy cycles; technological deflation compounds. AI’s cost-cutting leverage
doesn’t fade when the Fed hikes, it accelerates as models improve and adoption spreads.
The real story isn’t the revenue of the hyperscalers; it’s the margin expansion of everyone else. Just as QE redefined “fair
value” for assets in the 2010s, AI is redefining it for earnings in the 2020s.
QE delayed creative destruction; AI accelerates it.
The debt that once bought time is now a weight that slows transformation. In the age of computational liquidity, survival
depends not on refinancing capacity but on reinvention capacity. The zombies of the ZIRP decade won’t get a second
resurrection, this time, the liquidity is earned, not printed.
We are shifting from a decade of financial liquidity to a decade of computational liquidity, from borrowing returns to
building them. If QE inflated markets through debt, AI will inflate them through efficiency. And as the cost of intelligence
keeps falling, the true era of synthetic growth has only just begun.

131449:
November 13, 2025

CoreWeave’s Warning: When AI Demand Meets Infrastructure Reality
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

The Supply-Constrained Revolution
It has been a fun year writing about AI in 2025. The AI boom has reshaped global markets over the past three years,
caused constant fears of a bubble and has driven record capital expenditures, surging equity valuations, and a historic
demand for compute power. Staying bullish on the AI infrastructure trade in 2025 has been the way to make money but it
has not been easy. Based on CoreWeave’s latest update this week, I think the next phase of this revolution will be very
different for investors: the constraint has moved from capital to concrete and with it, I expect a very different year in 2026.
Although I believe 2026 will be another year where the winners will be the ones benefitting from the acceleration in AI, I
also believe the sector and name winners will be very different than the prior three years and Coreweave was a warning
sign of a transition. Next year for me is about sectors about to benefit from AI adoption and one where the massive
buildout means a broadening out PMIs heading higher. With more rising estimate revision competition and less
concentration, the bar for AI legacy infrastructure winners will be higher.
The artificial intelligence buildout has reached an inflection point where the constraint is no longer demand, capital
availability, or technological capability, it’s physical infrastructure delivery. CoreWeave’s earnings call provided the clearest
signal yet that the AI industry is running into hard infrastructure bottlenecks that will reshape investment performance
across the sector in 2026 and beyond. When do you hear a company say they have “insatiable” demand to fill and the
stock falls 16% that day.
But here’s what makes this moment so unusual: while AI is advancing, the macro picture feels like we are coming out of a
recession. The Fed is cutting rates because of weakness in the jobs market, credit stress is rising in auto loans, housing,
and credit cards. Oil prices are down 15% YTD. These are classic signs of economic weakness yet infrastructure
bottlenecks are tightening, not loosening. In a normal cycle, Fed rate cuts stimulate demand to fill excess capacity. Right
now in the fastest growing part of the economy and stock market, there is no excess capacity. Companies are rationing
customers, cutting capex guidance not from lack of demand but from construction delays, and equipment manufacturers
are sold out through 2028.
This is the K-shaped economy in real time. At the bottom of the K, consumers face credit stress and rising unemployment
fears, conditions that would typically trigger recession. Yet the story is circular: AI and other exponential innovations are
driving margin expansion, which in turn reshapes labor demand and market concentration. The stock market’s rally
increases household net worth but at the same time AI creates labor anxiety that is growing by the day. At the top of the K,
The Mag 7 enjoy AI-driven margin gains and are investing over $400 billion annually in infrastructure they can’t yet
monetize. The Fed can stimulate consumers, but it can’t accelerate transformer production or data center construction.
Perhaps the “AI bubble” critics are asking the wrong question. The issue isn’t return on invested capital. CoreWeave’s
85% backlog growth and Oracle’s $455 billion in contracted revenue prove the returns are real. The bubble is in TIME: the
gap between exponential demand growth and linear infrastructure buildout capacity. When equipment lead times stretch
to 2-7 years and data center construction faces 12-24 month delays while token demand is growing exponentially, even
perfect execution cannot close the gap between what customers want and what the physical economy can deliver.

 CoreWeave slashed its 2025 capital expenditure guidance by roughly 40% from $20-23 billion down to $12-14 billion not
because demand weakened or financing became unavailable, but because a third-party data center developer failed to
deliver “power shells” on schedule. This wasn’t a demand problem. The company’s revenue backlog surged 85% quarterover-quarter to $55.6 billion, driven by contracts with Meta, OpenAI, and Nvidia. Management described customer
requests for compute resources as “far exceeding current capacity” and characterized demand as “insatiable.”
CoreWeave has the capital, the customers, and the orders but it cannot deploy the infrastructure fast enough. As
management stated bluntly, “This is a systemic problem that the industry will have to deal with for the foreseeable future.”
This admission marks a regime change in how investors should think about AI infrastructure investments.
Oracle Confirms the Pattern: Signed Contracts, Missing Infrastructure
CoreWeave’s experience is not isolated. Oracle with its $657 billion market cap and a staggering $455 billion revenue
backlog faces identical constraints. CEO Safra Catz acknowledged in June 2025: “We actually currently are still waving off
customers or scheduling them out into the future so that we have enough supply to meet demand… We are putting out as
much capacity as we possibly can.”
Oracle is turning away customers despite having secured massive contracts with OpenAI, Meta, and xAI. The bottleneck
isn’t demand, capital, or technology. As CEO Mike Sicilia stated in October 2025: “There is real value in artificial
intelligence as demand far outpaces supply.” Sicilia explicitly dismissed “AI bubble” fears, recognizing that compute
supply, power availability, and physical facilities cannot keep pace with AI workload growth.
Industry analysts covering Oracle’s earnings identified the core constraint: “This is due to supply constraints across land,
buildings, energy and GPUs.” Another review was even more direct: “Execution bottleneck: Oracle signed the contracts,
but now it has to actually build the data centers… Execution now is about power, GPUs, and the cadence of embedded
regions with AWS, Azure, and Google.”
Oracle’s situation mirrors CoreWeave’s perfectly both companies have secured enormous customer commitments, both
have available capital, and both are constrained by the physical buildout timeline. The pattern is now undeniable: the AI
infrastructure industry has transitioned from capital-constrained to delivery-constrained. And the similarities extend to the
recent performance for their stocks.
GE Vernova: The Equipment Bottleneck Revealed
The infrastructure constraint extends beyond data center shells and power availability. It reaches into the physical
equipment supply chain itself. GE Vernova (GEV), the dominant supplier of gas turbines, transformers, and grid
equipment for AI data centers, has made the bottleneck explicit.
CEO Scott Strazik stated in March 2025 at CERAWeek: “I would expect by the end of the summer, we will be largely sold
out through the end of ’28 with this equipment” referring to gas turbines, power transformers, and switchgear. The
company currently has 50 GW of gas turbines under contract or slot reservation, with lead times stretching into 20282030.
But Strazik’s most revealing commentary came in a June 2025 LinkedIn post where he laid out the transformer crisis:
Distribution transformers now take up to 2 years to deliver, a 4× increase over pre-2022 levels
Large power transformers average 120 weeks, with some orders stretching to 210 weeks (4 years)
Lead times for gas turbines now range from 3 to 7 years, depending on model
His summary was blunt: “No transformer = no project.”
GE Vernova purchases nearly $20 billion in materials and components sourced from over 100 countries. The company
faces “supply-chain strains” that have caused its Wind division orders to fall 43% year-over-year as “supply chain
bottlenecks and project delays persist.” RBC analyst Chris Dendrinos noted that GE Vernova’s recent $41 million
2

 investment in generator capacity was necessary to “prevent additional bottlenecks” because generators are “a crucial
complement to GE Vernova’s gas turbine business.”
Notably, Strazik told investors in January 2025: “There is significant growth we will achieve in this facility in the years
ahead, all within the same four walls, without pouring concrete or adding new cranes.” This suggests GE Vernova is
intentionally limiting capacity expansion to maintain margins, a strategy that keeps the bottleneck tight and prices high, but
does nothing to solve the industry’s capacity constraint.
From GPU Scarcity to Grid Constraints
For the past two years, the AI investment narrative centered on GPU availability. Nvidia’s H100 and H200 chips were the
binding constraint, creating a clear winner-takes-all dynamic that drove Nvidia’s market capitalization above $3 trillion.
That bottleneck has largely resolved. CoreWeave explicitly stated the market remains “supply-constrained across all
generations of GPU compute,” but the constraint has shifted downstream.
The new chokepoints are gas turbines, electrical transformers, grid interconnection capacity, and now, with CoreWeave’s
guidance cut, Oracle’s customer rationing, and GE Vernova’s sold-out order book reveal the actual physical construction
of data center shells and the manufacturing capacity for power equipment. Industry observers noted that “more graphics
processing units and data centers could alleviate current bottlenecks in the AI training and inference process,” but the
emphasis has shifted decisively toward the data center, power delivery, and equipment manufacturing components of that
equation.
These are not software problems that can be solved with better algorithms or more funding. These are multi-year civil
engineering, manufacturing, and regulatory challenges involving physical supply chains, utility coordination, and permitting
processes. When Oracle’s CFO is “waving off customers,” CoreWeave is pushing revenue recognition into 2026 because
construction partners missed timelines, and GE Vernova is sold out through 2028, the message is clear: the digital
economy’s expansion rate has exceeded the physical economy’s ability to respond.
The Backlog Paradox: $500 Billion in Contracts, Nowhere to Deploy Them
The combined revenue backlogs of CoreWeave ($55.6 billion) and Oracle ($455 billion) total over half a trillion dollars and
these are just two players in a market that includes Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, each deploying $50-100 billion
annually in AI capex. This creates what we might call the “backlog paradox”: unprecedented customer demand and
contracted revenue, but no clear path to convert those contracts into actual infrastructure within reasonable timeframes.
Oracle’s situation is particularly instructive because the company has advantages CoreWeave lacks with decades of data
center experience, established utility relationships, massive balance sheet capacity, and strategic partnerships with AWS,
Azure, and Google Cloud for “embedded regions.” Yet even Oracle is constrained by what analysts called “power
timelines” and “contract terms” around energy availability.
GE Vernova’s situation adds another dimension: even if you have the land, the power agreements, and the capital, you
still face 2-4 year lead times on transformers and 3-7 year lead times on gas turbines. The supply chain bottleneck is
comprehensive and it spans data center construction, power generation equipment, and grid interconnection
infrastructure.
If Oracle, with its institutional knowledge, capital resources, and hyperscaler partnerships, is rationing customers due to
supply constraints, and if GE Vernova, the dominant equipment supplier, is sold out through 2028, what does that imply
for smaller operators, new entrants, or companies dependent on third-party developers? The answer is visible in
CoreWeave’s 40% capex guidance cut: execution risk is now the dominant variable in AI infrastructure valuation.
The paradox at the heart of this AI revolution is that its very success has become its greatest vulnerability. In my opinion,
the next AI investment phase will not be defined by who can spend the most, but by who can execute through constraint.
Investors must now distinguish between companies that can monetize their backlog and those merely accumulating one.
The shift from a capital-constrained to a delivery-constrained world means that execution risk, project management, and
physical infrastructure competency, not raw innovation, will drive relative performance. In 2026, the winners will be those
who convert contracts into capacity while preserving margins amid rising costs and tighter timelines.
3

 In my upcoming video this weekend and my written piece next week, I’ll explore the next major frontier: AI’s impact on
Pharma. In my view, this will mark the moment when AI’s economic influence moves beyond infrastructure and into the
lifeblood of global growth. If realized, the integration of AI in drug discovery and healthcare could permanently alter the
trajectory of longevity, corporate earnings and economic productivity, becoming the defining AI story of 2026 and beyond.
This moment marks a regime change not just in the AI economy but in market structure itself. When time, not capital,
becomes the scarcest asset, valuation multiples tied to near-term earnings optimism become fragile. The market’s
leadership will broaden as downstream sectors, construction, electrical equipment, and industrial automation, benefit from
the physical buildout cycle, even as pure-play AI beneficiaries face tougher comparisons and delayed revenue realization.
CoreWeave’s warning is not about fading demand but about the limits of exponential growth colliding with the linear world
of concrete, copper, and kilowatts. The next great trade will come from understanding that the bottlenecks themselves,
time, transformers, and transmission, are the new sources of scarcity premium in the age of artificial intelligence.

4
