155485:
June 4, 2026

The Fireworks Show Is Over: Agentic AI Moves From Discovery to
Digestion
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

One of the hardest things for investors to recognize during an exponential technological transition is the difference
between new information and delayed understanding. Markets do not always move because the facts have changed.
Often, they move because investors finally accept facts that had been hiding in plain sight. When you combine that
delayed recognition with unprecedented revenue and earnings reality, you get parabolic moves. The intersection of being
unprepared and then being hit with a historic surprise can be explosive. That is what appears to have happened in the first
five months of the year. The AI story did not radically change between January, March, and June. What changed was the
sell-side and buy-side willingness to believe it.
As we approach the 250th birthday of America, the best way to think about this period is through the image of a fireworks
show. At first, the sky is dark. People are waiting, but they are not yet sure what they are going to see. Then the first
explosion lights up the sky. A few people notice the shape. Then more fireworks follow, each one confirming the pattern.
By the finale, everyone can see it. The show is spectacular, but once it is over, the surprise is gone. We move on until the
next fireworks show.
That is where I think we are in AI. The fireworks show is over, and the last couple of weeks have been the climax of an
incredible display. But the agentic AI buildout has just begun.
I have covered this journey in my writings and in the conversations I have had with people. Whether it was debating
inference, tokens, and memory last year against the cries of ROIC or bubble, or watching everyone finally get there, the
pattern has been the same. Whether it was the hate mail after my Marvell paper in March or the two-month echo chamber
of “How can I buy these up here?” as earnings season began, the last two weeks have felt to me like the climax of a
fireworks show. Maybe this is my time to be caught going against the exponential tide, but I think the warning signs are
clear. More people are now fighting against the bottleneck and shortage side than against the agentic AI buildout itself.
For me, that usually means the all-you-can-eat AI buffet has left everyone full and in need of a digestion period.
To begin the story of why, let’s go through my version of the investor journey toward realizing the importance of agentic AI.
It can be seen through three major speaking gatherings in the AI world: Jensen Huang’s presentation at the Consumer
Electronics Show, the Morgan Stanley Technology Conference in March, and the most recent Computex gathering in
Taiwan. Each event told the same story with slightly more confirmation. At CES, the message was still early and
uncomfortable for many investors. At Morgan Stanley, it became broader and more institutional, with actual numbers from
the companies. By Computex, it had become consensus. The market did not discover a new AI world in June. It spent the
first five months of the year catching up to the agentic world Jensen had already described in January.
At CES, Jensen effectively laid out the next phase of AI infrastructure. The key point was not simply that NVIDIA had
another chip cycle. The key point was that Vera Rubin represented a new architecture for a different kind of workload.
Investors were still largely thinking about AI through the lens of training large language models and scaling data centers
for chatbots. The mental model was still “more GPUs for more ChatGPT.” But the world Jensen was describing was much
more dynamic. It was about inference, agents, tokens, memory, networking, latency, power efficiency, and the movement
of data back and forth through optical fiber across massive AI factories. He followed up CES at Davos a couple of weeks

 later, where he emphasized, in an interview with Larry Fink, the now-understood five-layer cake for the $90 trillion AI
buildout.
That distinction around inference matters. A chatbot is episodic. You ask a question, it gives an answer, and the session
ends. What everyone learned after OpenAI was that an agentic system is persistent. It plans, reasons, remembers, uses
tools, calls other models, interacts with software, and keeps working. That creates a completely different infrastructure
problem. It is not just about training a smarter model once. It is about serving intelligence continuously. The bottleneck
shifts from pure compute to system throughput. Tokens per watt matter. Networking matters. Optical connections matter.
Memory matters. Storage matters. Latency matters. The entire factory matters. Investors were still questioning the
importance of chatbots while missing the transition to the agentic rise that had begun in late 2025.
This is why Vera Rubin was so important. It was not just a new product announcement. It was a map of the world NVIDIA
believed was coming. The platform was designed around the reality that agentic AI would create enormous demand for
inference and system-level coordination. Investors who were still debating whether AI was a chatbot bubble were being
shown the early blueprint for an economy where software starts acting on behalf of humans. In hindsight, CES was not
the beginning of the story. It was the first flare in the sky.
My view is that investors were not ready. They heard the words, but they did not fully process the implications. They were
still anchored to the prior phase of AI. They understood the data center buildout, but they had not yet internalized the
traffic patterns of an agentic world. They understood GPUs, but the parabolic rise of GPU demand was seen with one
company. The agentic rise brought a broadening out of beneficiaries. Investors had not yet focused enough on memory
bandwidth, optical interconnects, networking, storage, and power. They understood model training, but they had not yet
shifted their thinking to always-on inference. In January, the message was there. The market was still catching up, and
due to many companies already moving in parabolas, the cries of bubble were loud. Market bubblistas need many
examples rather than one GPU company at a 10+ bagger over three years.
By the time we got to the Morgan Stanley conference in March, just ahead of earnings season, the story had expanded. It
was no longer just Jensen Huang making the case. The broader technology ecosystem was now reinforcing it. Leaders
from NVIDIA, AMD, Intel, Dell, and other infrastructure companies were all discussing AI not as a narrow chip cycle but as
a full-stack infrastructure transition. The language had shifted from models to systems, from training to inference, from
software tools to agents, and from data centers to AI factories. This was the second stage of recognition.
That mattered because investors often need repetition from multiple sources before they are willing to reprice a theme.
One CEO can sound promotional. One keynote can be dismissed as visionary. But when the same message appears
across semiconductors, cloud platforms, software companies, networking providers, and hardware manufacturers, it
becomes harder to ignore. Morgan Stanley gave investors the ecosystem confirmation that CES had only started to
reveal.
The key realization was that the agentic world was not theoretical. It was already being built into product roadmaps,
capital spending plans, supply agreements, and customer conversations. The implications were enormous. If agents
become the next interface for software, then compute demand does not merely grow linearly. It becomes tied to activity.
Every workflow becomes a potential source of token demand. Every enterprise application becomes a possible agent
platform. Every person could eventually have multiple digital workers operating in the background. That means AI
infrastructure is not a one-time buildout. It becomes a recurring industrial system.
That is why the March conference was so important for the market. It broadened the narrative from “NVIDIA has a new
platform” to “the entire technology industry is organizing itself around the agentic transition.” The investor base began to
recognize that the winners would not only be the obvious GPU companies. The opportunity set included networking,
optical, memory, storage, power, cooling, servers, security, edge devices, and software platforms that could monetize
agentic workflows. The market began to reprice the picks and shovels of the new intelligence economy.
Then came Computex in Taiwan this past week. What was most interesting for me about Computex was not how much
changed. It was how little changed. The same core message was repeated again. Vera Rubin. AI factories. Agentic AI.
Local AI PCs. Inference. Networking. Power efficiency. Taiwan as the center of the supply chain. The world presented at
Computex was the same world described earlier in the year. The difference was that investors were now ready for it. What
sounded aggressive in January sounded obvious by June.
2

 That is a crucial shift. At CES, the market was still learning the language. At Morgan Stanley, the market was translating
the language into earnings estimates. At Computex, the market already knew the language. That means the burden of
proof has changed. Earlier in the year, companies could surprise investors simply by confirming that agentic AI demand
was real. Now the market assumes it is real. Earlier in the year, the upside came from recognition. Now the upside has to
come from execution.
This is where the fireworks analogy becomes useful. In January, the sky was still dark. Jensen’s CES presentation was
the first major burst of light. In March, the Morgan Stanley conference added more explosions, showing that the pattern
was not isolated to one company. By June, Computex became the finale. The sky was fully lit. Everyone could finally see
the same picture. But once everyone sees it, the market changes. The crowd stops being amazed and starts asking what
comes next.
But Computex itself is not the finale for me. There have been other signs, and that is why the recent market evidence is so
important. Broadcom reported very strong AI-related results last night, but the stock still underwhelmed because
expectations had already moved. That is what happens when a theme goes from underappreciated to widely accepted.
Good news is no longer enough. Investors begin asking whether growth is faster than expected, whether guidance is
being raised enough, whether supply is available soon enough, whether margins can hold, and whether the next leg of
demand is already reflected in the price.
Broadcom was not an isolated example. Cerebras came public with enormous fanfare because it represented one of the
clearest public-market expressions of the search for alternatives and complements to the GPU model, particularly with
regard to memory. In a world where memory bandwidth, inference speed, and system architecture are becoming central
bottlenecks, the IPO attracted attention because it fit directly into the market’s new obsession: not just who wins AI, but
who solves the infrastructure constraints created by inference and agents. It has not traded well post-IPO. At almost the
same time, Google moved to raise roughly $85 billion to fund its AI ambitions, a financing amount larger than the market
value of a majority of S&P 500 companies. I will add that Berkshire Hathaway, which has been raising cash throughout the
last few years, bought $10 billion of a company that has more than doubled over the last year. Really, now? That can only
be late agentic understanding. Then came the continued wave of trillion-dollar-plus AI capex-related IPO announcements,
the reality of Vera Rubin moving from roadmap in January to launch, and the completion of another earnings season in
which every major company had the opportunity to update investors on AI demand. Now that is a finale!
Most importantly for me, this investor acceptance and the obvious capital needs are now combined with the reality of
bottlenecks, shortages, hoarding, dramatic price increases, and the continued uncertainty around the Strait of Hormuz. It
is simple: the odds have changed since CES. Stocks are up. Sentiment no longer doubts. Signs of “easy money” being
made are showing up in Korea, while many of the surprises and new pieces of information are now appearing on the other
side of the agentic reality. At a minimum, I see a period of two-sided consolidation needed to burn off the excesses and
bring sentiment back to a place of balance. If the Strait of Hormuz follows the expectations of prediction markets, with less
than a 50% chance of normal flows before October, I think the surprise will now be about which part of the agentic
buildout suffers the most. This is a complex buildout, and if any one part sees delays, expect it to show up across many of
the companies.
When you put all of these events together, and add in the divergences I have been highlighting in the sectors related to AI
and factor volatility, the message to me is clear: the inference buildout is no longer a hidden or misunderstood theme. It is
now broadly recognized, broadly financed, and much more fairly reflected in prices than it was at the start of the year. The
first five months were not just about AI getting bigger. They were about investors rapidly responding to the shift from chatbased AI to agentic AI. The market did not slowly adjust. It sprinted. As my father taught me, the odds on the tote board
are now fair, so let’s sit out this race until we see better odds.
This does not mean the AI cycle is over. I believe the agentic thematic portfolio I put together will continue to outperform in
the months and years to come. However, I believe the joy of watching the fireworks show is over, and now the hard work
of navigating a more challenging long/short environment, with a significant macro supply chain risk ahead of midterm
elections, will be an overhang to expectations. The agentic buildout continues. The real economic impact of agentic AI
may still be in its earliest stages. Enterprises are only beginning to understand how digital workers will change workflows.
Consumers are only beginning to understand what always-on personal AI will mean. The hardware supply chain is still

3

 scaling. The power grid is still adapting. The software layer is still being rewritten. The long-term transformation remains
enormous.
But the stock market is not the technology. The technology can keep accelerating while the stocks become more twosided. That is the point investors need to understand now. In January, disbelief created opportunity. By June, belief had
created a higher bar. Earlier in the year, investors were paid to recognize the agentic shift before others did. Going
forward, they will need to separate the companies that can actually deliver from the companies merely attached to the
story. I am in a good seat to hear and see the sentiment shift, and we are at a very different place today.
We have entered a different phase of the AI trade. The first phase was about whether AI was real. The second phase was
about whether infrastructure spending would be large enough to matter. The third phase was about whether agentic AI
would require a new architecture. Those questions have largely been answered. The next phase is about expectations,
timing, bottlenecks, and valuation.
That makes the market more challenging. It is now equally possible for companies to underwhelm as it is for them to
overwhelm. A company can report extraordinary growth and still disappoint. A company can be central to the AI buildout
and still see its stock fall. A company can have the right long-term story but the wrong short-term setup. That is what
happens when prices catch up to the narrative.
The lesson from CES to Morgan Stanley to Computex is not that the AI story changed. The lesson is that investor
perception changed. The agentic world was introduced in January, validated in March, and accepted by June. That
acceptance is bullish for the long-term transformation but more complicated for the stocks. The easy part of the repricing
may be behind us. From here, the winners will need to prove not only that the agentic world is coming, but that they can
translate that world into revenue, margins, supply, and earnings faster than the market already expects.
The fireworks show is over. The market has seen the light. Now comes the harder part: digestion, execution, and
selectivity.
That is the new setup. AI has moved from hidden to known, from discovery to digestion, from skepticism to consensus.
The buildout is still real. The agentic world is still coming. But the market has caught up to the story. Now the story has to
keep outrunning the market.

155685:
June 5, 2026

Spotlight: The GLP-1 Manhattan Project: How Eli Lilly Is Using Today’s
Metabolic Cash Flow to Build Tomorrow’s Biological Intelligence
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

These numbers are shocking. It’s like we got a new frontier AI model but for the body. – Bryan Johnson regarding Eli
Lilly’s recent retatrutide data
I want to make sure my feelings on the importance of this paper are clear with some extreme hot takes to start. First, I
believe by the end of this decade, Eli Lilly could be the largest company in the US. Second, Eli Lilly is the becoming the
most important AI company in the world. Third, peptides this decade are equivalent to API keys from the prior decade.
Hopefully that got your attention enough to read what is the longest paper I have written in a long time and this is after I
cut it down by 50%. It took almost a year of research to gather the thoughts and information to support these hot takes.
If you go back and look at history, every major technology cycle begins with a breakthrough product, but the companies
that define the cycle and the product rarely stop there. The breakthrough gives them revenue. Revenue gives them
capital. Capital gives them the ability to build infrastructure. Infrastructure gives them the ability to compound. We are
watching that now with the hyperscalers as they race to win the next technology cycle built on AI.
In my opinion, that is the story now unfolding at Eli Lilly.
Most investors understand Lilly and its recent success through the lens of GLP-1s. Mounjaro and Zepbound which have
transformed the company from a successful pharmaceutical business into one of the most important growth companies in
the world. The GLP-1 market has become a commercial phenomenon because it sits at the center of obesity, diabetes,
metabolic dysfunction, cardiovascular risk, sleep apnea, inflammation, and potentially many other diseases tied to modern
health. It hurt consumer staples companies and recently was impactful to Victoria’s Secret’s earnings and stock price.
Focusing only on Lilly’s current revenue misses the larger strategic point.
GLP-1s are the funding engine for Lilly’s next act.
That next act is artificial intelligence for human biology.
The most important thing happening at Lilly is the way cash flow from the GLP-1 franchise is giving the company the
balance sheet, ambition, data and urgency to build a specialized AI infrastructure stack for drug discovery, genetic
medicine, manufacturing, and eventually longevity. While the world is focused on general-purpose LLM companies racing
to build ever-broader intelligence models looking for consumer and enterprise adoption while fighting commoditization risk
and explosive rising costs for the infrastructure, Lilly is building something narrower and potentially far more defensible: a
proprietary biological intelligence system trained on decades of molecular, preclinical, clinical, manufacturing, and patient
knowledge.
General AI vs. Biological AI
The general AI race is horizontal. OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, Meta, xAI and China are competing to build models that can
reason across everything: software, writing, coding, math, images, video, research, enterprise work, consumer interfaces,
and eventually autonomous agents. It is a massive race, and it is also a crowded race that is deflationary on one side and

 costly on the buildout. The same basic ingredients are available to many competitors: capital, GPUs, talent, synthetic
data, public data, enterprise data, and distribution.
Lilly’s race is different. It is vertical. It is specialized. It is domain-specific. It is built around biology, chemistry, medicine,
and the industrial process of turning scientific knowledge into approved therapies. This model is designed to ask better
biological questions, design better molecules, run better experiments, predict safety and efficacy earlier, manufacture
more efficiently, and shorten the time between hypothesis and medicine.
That is a fundamentally different business model than building a chatbot that turns into agents.
It is closer to building a new operating system for the human body.
The reason GLP-1 revenue is so important is that this type of infrastructure cannot be built cheaply. It requires compute,
data, robotics, laboratories, scientists, engineers, partnerships, acquisitions, regulatory knowledge, manufacturing scale,
and patience. Many AI-native biotech companies have exciting models, but they do not have Lilly’s cash flow. Many
technology companies have compute, but they do not have Lilly’s proprietary biological data. Many pharmaceutical
companies have data, but they may not have Lilly’s current growth engine or urgency.
Lilly sits at a unique intersection: massive revenue growth today, massive disease markets tomorrow which currently
make up close to 20% of consumer expenditures, and a strategic need to convert current dominance into long-term
infrastructure.
That is why the Manhattan Project analogy works.
The Manhattan Project was a concentrated mobilization of capital, talent, physics, engineering, manufacturing, and
national urgency around one problem. Lilly’s AI effort has a very different purpose, but the structure of the analogy is
useful. This is an effort to assemble the pieces of a new scientific production system. Data, compute, AI agents, wet labs,
dry labs, robotics, genetic medicine, and manufacturing are being pulled into one loop. The goal is to make discovery
faster, cheaper, more predictive, and more continuous.
From Drug Discovery to a Scientific Production System
The old drug discovery model was slow because biology is complex and the experimental cycle is painful. A scientist
forms a hypothesis. A molecule is designed. Experiments are run. Data comes back. Teams analyze it. New hypotheses
are formed. The process repeats, often for years. Most compounds fail. Many failures occur late, after enormous cost and
time have already been spent. The industry has always lived with this attrition because biology is hard.
AI changes the bottleneck.
The first phase of AI in drug discovery was mostly about prediction: predicting protein structures, molecular properties,
toxicity, binding affinity, drug-like characteristics, and disease pathways. That was useful, but still limited. The more
important phase is agentic AI. An AI agent can read literature, form hypotheses, choose tools, design experiments,
interact with robotic labs, analyze results, write reports, and begin the next cycle.
In other words, AI agents can help automate the Design-Make-Test-Analyze loop that sits at the heart of pharmaceutical
research.
This is where Lilly’s strategy becomes more powerful. A general LLM agent may help with coding, emails, research, or
office workflows. A Lilly agent connected to proprietary molecular data, safety data, preclinical data, wet labs, robotics,
and manufacturing workflows becomes part of the scientific process itself. The agent participates in the creation of new
biological knowledge.
That is a profound difference.
Failure Becomes Training Data

2

 Lilly’s data is the real moat in biological AI. The internet created a massive training set for general models. Biology does
not have the same open training set. The highest-value data sits inside pharmaceutical companies, academic labs, clinical
trials, failed experiments, manufacturing systems, toxicology studies, and patient records. Much of it is messy, expensive,
proprietary, and difficult to interpret.
You cannot scrape your way into Lilly’s history of molecules. You cannot easily recreate decades of experimental failure.
And in drug discovery, failure is training data.
That is one of the most underappreciated ideas in the AI-pharma story. The pharmaceutical industry has spent decades
generating enormous amounts of negative information. Compounds that did not work. Targets that failed. Doses that were
unsafe. Pathways that looked promising but did not translate. Trials that disappointed. Historically, much of that failure
was viewed as cost. In an AI world, it becomes an asset.
A model that knows what failed, why it failed, and under what conditions it failed has a better chance of avoiding the same
mistakes. The graveyard of failed drug discovery may become one of the most valuable datasets in the world.
This is also why Lilly’s strategy is more defensible than the general LLM race. In the LLM world, many companies can
train on similar public data, license similar datasets, generate synthetic data, and chase similar benchmarks. In pharma,
the most important benchmark is whether the model can help create a molecule that works in the human body, avoids
unacceptable toxicity, can be manufactured, can pass regulatory review, and can become a medicine.
That requires a closed loop between model and reality.
The lab is the judge. The patient is the ultimate judge.
TuneLab: The Distributed Learning Network
However, most of what I have written so far is from a top-down perspective. The part that has gathered my focus is the
same as the ones for memory, optical, power, the rack, edge and advanced semiconductor packaging. The partnerships
and actual announcements happening right now. AI moves at an exponential pace and so the companies also have to
connect at an exponential pace with the ecosystem and this has been the driving factor for Eli Lilly starting with TuneLab.
Lilly’s TuneLab is one of the clearest signs of this direction. On the surface, TuneLab looks like a generous platform: Lilly
gives biotechnology companies access to AI-enabled drug discovery models trained on decades of Lilly research data
and built through more than $1 billion of research investment. Strategically, TuneLab is much more than a tool. It is a giveto-get strategy. Lilly is trading access to its AI models for something potentially more valuable over time: participation in a
broader biological learning network.
The key concept is federated learning. In a traditional data model, companies would have to pool their data in one central
location. That is nearly impossible in biotech because the most valuable data is proprietary, sensitive, regulated, and
competitively guarded. Federated learning changes the structure. The model can learn across distributed datasets while
the raw data remains inside each participant’s environment. The partner keeps control of its data, while the shared model
improves from the patterns, outcomes, and experimental feedback generated across the network.
That matters because the bottleneck in biological AI is data diversity as much as compute. A single pharmaceutical
company, even one as large as Lilly, cannot internally explore every chemical space, every target, every disease pathway,
every modality, and every experimental failure. A network of biotechs can. Each partner may be working on a different
target, a different mechanism, a different molecule class, or a different therapeutic area. Each one is generating data Lilly
would never have produced internally.
TuneLab creates a structure where those distributed efforts can improve the intelligence of the shared system.
This is the data flywheel. Lilly’s historical data makes the initial models useful. That usefulness attracts biotech partners.
Those partners apply the models to their own proprietary programs. Their experiments generate new learning. The model
improves. A better model attracts more users. More users generate more diverse learning. The system compounds.
3

 That is why TuneLab should be viewed as an outsourced R&D network. TuneLab sits inside Lilly Catalyze360, the
company’s broader venture, partnering, and incubation platform. That gives Lilly a front-row seat to emerging founders,
targets, modalities, and therapeutic strategies before they are obvious to the public market. It can build relationships with
companies before they reach major clinical milestones. It can see where scientific energy is moving. It can identify which
approaches are gaining traction. And it can create a natural funnel for future licensing deals, joint ventures, partnerships,
or acquisitions. Think Google X.
The Google X comparison is useful, but only partially. Like Alphabet’s moonshot model, Lilly is creating a sandbox where
high-risk, early-stage innovation can form around its infrastructure. Google X was largely designed to find Alphabet’s next
business beyond search and advertising. TuneLab is designed to reinforce Lilly’s existing core business.
That makes TuneLab less like a moonshot factory and more like a biological data flywheel. Lilly is using its scale to
become part of the platform layer for early-stage drug discovery. If enough biotechs use Lilly’s models to screen
compounds, predict toxicity, evaluate pharmacokinetics, and prioritize candidates, then Lilly’s parameters begin to
influence what the ecosystem considers a promising molecule.
In the broader AI arms race, algorithms will eventually commoditize. Proprietary data, specialized workflows, compute
infrastructure, and feedback loops are the constraints. TuneLab is Lilly’s attempt to own more of that loop. It turns outside
innovation into inside learning. It turns smaller biotech companies into distributed nodes in a larger biological intelligence
system. And it gives Lilly a structural advantage that can scale beyond the limits of its own R&D headcount.
The clean investor line is this: TuneLab is Lilly’s attempt to turn the biotech ecosystem into a distributed learning network
or a way to make outside innovation improve Lilly’s inside intelligence.
External AI Partnerships: Buying Speed, Optionality, and Frontier Methods
TuneLab shows that Lilly wants to turn the broader biotech ecosystem into a distributed learning network. Lilly is also
building relationships with some of the most important AI-native drug discovery companies in the world.
That matters because the winner in biological AI may be the company that knows which external tools to integrate, which
platforms to partner with, and which discoveries to pull into its own development and commercialization machine.
The partnerships with Isomorphic Labs and Insilico Medicine are important signals.
Isomorphic Labs is especially meaningful because it comes from the DeepMind and AlphaFold lineage. AlphaFold
changed the world’s understanding of protein structure prediction. Isomorphic is trying to apply that same AI-native
mindset to drug design. Lilly’s collaboration with Isomorphic focuses on discovering small-molecule therapeutics against
undisclosed targets. Strategically, that gives Lilly exposure to one of the most advanced AI drug-design engines in the
world without needing to own all of that capability internally.
Insilico Medicine represents a different but equally important layer. Insilico has built a full-stack AI drug discovery platform
spanning target discovery, molecule generation, and clinical-development prediction. Lilly’s expanded relationship with
Insilico suggests that the company is willing to use external AI engines as sources of actual drug candidates. That is an
important shift. Lilly is using AI-native companies as upstream engines for future pipeline formation.
This is the right strategy for a pharmaceutical company with Lilly’s scale. In general AI, the frontier model companies often
try to own the entire stack because the model itself is the product. In specialized biological AI, the medicine is the final
product. Lilly does not need to build every model, every interface, or every discovery engine itself. It needs to control the
highest-value parts of the loop: proprietary data, capital allocation, clinical development, regulatory execution,
manufacturing, distribution, and commercialization.
That is why these partnerships should be viewed as ROIC accelerators. Isomorphic gives Lilly access to frontier structural
biology and small-molecule design. Insilico gives Lilly access to generative chemistry, target discovery, and AI-created
programs. TuneLab gives Lilly a distributed network of biotech learning. NVIDIA gives Lilly the compute and physical AI
infrastructure. Lilly’s own data gives the system its proprietary memory.

4

 Together, these pieces turn Lilly into an aggregator of biological intelligence.
The broader point is simple: Lilly is building a portfolio of intelligence sources. Some are internal. Some are partnered.
Some are ecosystem-based. Some are embedded in external AI-native companies. This lowers the risk that Lilly misses
the winning method while increasing the chance that an important breakthrough in AI drug discovery can eventually flow
into Lilly’s development machine.
That is the strategic power of incumbency in the AI-pharma era. The AI-native companies may have the models. Lilly has
the cash flow, clinical infrastructure, regulatory knowledge, manufacturing scale, and commercial reach. By partnering
early, Lilly can turn external AI innovation into internal pipeline optionality.
Specialized AI Is an ROIC Story
There is another important difference between general AI and specialized AI.
The general AI companies are mostly selling a labor story. Their models are designed to replace, augment, or compress
human work across coding, customer service, research, legal workflows, sales, marketing, and back-office operations.
The promise is a world of digital employees.
Lilly’s specialized AI story is different. It is focused on improving the company’s own return on invested capital.
In pharma, the largest cost is failed capital: molecules that do not work, trials that end too late, manufacturing processes
that take too long to optimize, and biological hypotheses that consume years before reality proves them wrong. A
specialized biological AI model is valuable because it can help Lilly ask better questions, kill weak programs earlier,
design better molecules, run tighter experiments, and recycle every success and failure back into the system.
That is ROIC.
This also helps explain why secrecy may become more valuable. Lilly’s pipeline page now states that Phase 1 projects
are no longer disclosed. That change should not be ignored. Historically, early-stage pipeline disclosure helped investors
understand the breadth and direction of a pharmaceutical company’s research engine. In an AI-enabled world, early
disclosure may carry a higher competitive cost.
A Phase 1 asset can reveal a target, a mechanism, a biological pathway, a modality, a disease strategy, or a combination
logic that competitors can study with their own models. If AI reduces the time it takes rivals to learn from public
information, then public information becomes more dangerous. Lilly’s reduced disclosure therefore looks like a rational
response to a world where biological intelligence is becoming faster, more computational, and more competitive.
The message is subtle but important: the pipeline itself is becoming proprietary infrastructure.
LillyPod: The Infrastructure of Biological Intelligence
There has been one way to make money since the launch of ChatGPT and that is invest in Nvidia and its partners and
that’s where this story goes. Lilly’s NVIDIA infrastructure should be viewed not as IT, but as a new class of scientific
instrument. At the heart of this strategy is LillyPod, an NVIDIA DGX SuperPOD powered by over 1,000 NVIDIA Blackwell
Ultra GPUs. You have heard about AI factories. Well this is a big one. This is not a general-purpose cloud rental; it is a
wholly owned, industrial-scale AI factory designed to manage the entire lifecycle of biological discovery from data
ingestion and massive-scale molecular simulation to high-volume inference.
The shift here is from “artisanal” discovery to “industrialized” biology. While a productive wet-lab team might test roughly
2,000 molecular hypotheses per target, per year, LillyPod allows researchers to simulate and evaluate billions of
hypotheses in parallel. By integrating this hardware with the NVIDIA BioNeMo platform and a proprietary data stack built
on decades of experimental success and failure, Lilly is creating a closed-loop system where compute generates
hypotheses, robotics run the experiments, and the resulting data continuously improves the models.

5

 This infrastructure is the “bridge” between the world of bits and atoms: it enables the company to compress the biological
bottleneck by turning the scientific process into a continuous, compounding computational feedback loop.
This is pharmaceutical infrastructure becoming AI-native.
The NVIDIA co-innovation lab extends this even further. The strategic direction is to connect AI to physical
experimentation, robotics, manufacturing, and digital twins. That is the key step. In software, AI can improve quickly
because code can be written, tested, and deployed almost instantly. Biology has always been slower because
experiments require the physical world. Cells must grow. Assays must run. Animals or organoids may be studied.
Manufacturing processes must be validated. Clinical trials take time.
The promise of agentic wet labs and robotic experimentation is that the cycle time compresses dramatically.
This is where the “atoms to bits” idea becomes central. AI is exploding in the world of bits. Biology is still anchored in
atoms. The companies that win will be the ones that connect the two. Lilly is using GLP-1 cash flow to build the bridge
between digital intelligence and physical biology. Compute generates hypotheses. AI agents design experiments. Robotic
labs run them. Data returns to the model. The model improves. The next experiment begins.
Over time, that feedback loop becomes the asset.
Longevity Is a Network Problem
The longevity connection emerges naturally from this. Longevity is the interaction of many systems: metabolism,
cardiovascular function, inflammation, immune decline, neurodegeneration, muscle loss, mitochondrial function, cellular
senescence, genomic instability, and hormonal regulation. Traditional medicine has been organized around diseases.
Longevity forces medicine to think in networks. That makes it an ideal AI problem.
Lilly’s current GLP-1 franchise already touches one of the most important longevity pathways: metabolic health. Obesity
and diabetes increase the risk of cardiovascular disease, kidney disease, sleep apnea, liver disease, inflammation, and
many other health problems. Improving metabolic function may therefore have effects across healthspan, beyond weight
loss.
This is why the GLP-1 story is bigger than obesity.
It is about the possibility that one therapeutic class can improve multiple downstream systems tied to aging and chronic
disease.
GLP-1s may only be the beginning. If Lilly can use the revenue from metabolic disease to build an AI engine for broader
biology, the company can expand from treating chronic conditions to understanding the architecture of healthspan itself.
That does not mean immortality. It means identifying the biological bottlenecks that cause people to lose function, develop
disease, and age poorly. It means discovering interventions earlier. It means designing molecules faster. It means
combining therapies in more intelligent ways. It means moving from population averages toward more personalized
medicine.
The gene-editing angle strengthens this thesis. Lilly’s move into companies and partnerships focused on programmable
genetic medicine suggests that the company understands the next phase of medicine may extend beyond chronic
treatment. Some diseases may eventually be addressed through durable biological repair. A pill or injection taken
repeatedly manages a pathway. A gene-editing therapy may alter the underlying biology more permanently. AI-designed
tools for gene editing could become one of the most important areas of medicine because they expand what is
programmable inside the body.
In this sense, the future of pharma starts to look less like traditional drug development and more like software
development for biology.
The body is not software, and the analogy should not be pushed too far. Biology is messier, more adaptive, and more
dangerous than code. But the direction is clear. Medicine is moving from broad chemical intervention toward increasingly
6

 precise biological programming. GLP-1s modulate powerful metabolic pathways. Gene editors rewrite instructions. AI
agents help design the tools. Robotics accelerates experimentation. Digital twins simulate systems. Data compounds. The
entire process becomes more computational.
Peptides as API Keys for Human Software
This is where the comparison to the Mag 7 becomes useful.
The Mag 7 became valuable because they controlled the access layers of digital software. They owned the keys into
search, mobile operating systems, cloud infrastructure, commerce, identity, social graphs, enterprise workflows, and
accelerated compute. They controlled the points of access through which companies, consumers, developers,
advertisers, and enterprises interacted with the digital world.
Peptides are similar, but in biology.
They are access keys into human software.
The body is not software in the literal computer-code sense, but it behaves like a layered information system. DNA stores
instructions. RNA transmits messages. Proteins execute functions. Hormones coordinate signals. Receptors authenticate
those signals. Feedback loops regulate the entire system. A peptide fits into that system as a small programmable
sequence that can unlock a specific biological response.
In the digital world, an API key gives a developer permissioned access to a platform. In the biological world, a peptide,
hormone, or ligand gives a molecule access to a pathway. The software platform is the human biological system. The
codebase is DNA and cellular machinery. The authentication layer is receptor binding. The server response is the
physiological response. The permission structure is pathway specificity. The platform owner is the company with the data,
intellectual property, delivery mechanisms, manufacturing scale, and regulatory expertise to turn that biological access
into medicine.
GLP-1s are the perfect example.
A GLP-1 drug is essentially a biological API key. It plugs into existing human metabolic software and changes the
behavior of the system. It tells the body to feel fuller, slow gastric emptying, improve insulin response, reduce food noise,
alter energy balance, and potentially influence inflammation, cardiovascular risk, kidney disease, liver fat, addiction
pathways, and neurobiology.
That is why GLP-1s are bigger than obesity drugs.
They reveal that human software is programmable.
For the Mag 7, the market eventually learned that whoever controls the API layer controls the economics of the digital
world. For Lilly, Novo Nordisk, and the next generation of biotech platforms, the market may be learning something similar:
whoever controls the biological API layer may control the economics of future medicine.
The difference is depth.
The Mag 7 controlled access to external software: apps, devices, clouds, ads, payments, communication, and digital
workflows. Peptides control access to internal software: metabolism, appetite, inflammation, immunity, fertility, muscle,
aging, cognition, and cellular repair.
That makes the next platform shift potentially much larger. The first internet digitized information. The second wave
digitized commerce and communication. AI is digitizing labor and intelligence. Peptides, gene editing, and AI-designed
biology are beginning to digitize the human operating system itself.
If the Mag 7 became the API keys to the internet, peptides may become the API keys to the body. GLP-1s are the first
mass-market proof that human software can be accessed, modulated, and monetized at platform scale.

7

 That is the investor version of the Lilly thesis. The next great platform companies may control biological APIs, the keys
that let AI, data, and engineered molecules interface directly with human metabolism, disease, aging, and performance.
M&A as Buying More Biological API Keys
If peptides are API keys into human software, then Lilly’s M&A strategy should be viewed as an effort to buy more keys,
more pathways, more modalities, and more optionality.
On the surface, Lilly’s dealmaking looks like traditional pharma pipeline building. Large pharmaceutical companies have
always acquired assets, bought programs, licensed molecules, and partnered with smaller companies to replace future
revenue. But in Lilly’s case, the pace and nature of the activity suggest something larger. The company is using GLP-1
cash flow to buy speed, biological data, modality exposure, and strategic control across the next era of medicine.
This matters because AI does not eliminate the need for external innovation. It increases the value of knowing which
external innovation to buy.
If biological AI improves Lilly’s ability to evaluate targets, mechanisms, molecules, and modalities, then M&A becomes
more powerful. The company is no longer simply buying assets because they look promising in isolation. It can use its
internal data, AI models, clinical knowledge, and manufacturing expertise to decide which assets fit into a much larger
biological intelligence system. That turns M&A into a form of capital allocation enhanced by AI.
This is another reason the GLP-1 cash flow is so important. Lilly has the financial flexibility to act while the biological AI
landscape is still forming. It can buy gene-editing platforms, RNA-editing technologies, oncology programs, oral
therapeutics, inflammatory disease assets, vaccine developers, and AI-native discovery programs. Some of these deals
will become products. Some will become data. Some will become scientific knowledge. Some will become manufacturing
or modality capabilities. All of them can feed the machine.
That is the key point: in an AI-enabled pharma model, M&A is not only about buying revenue. It is about buying learning.
Verve gives Lilly exposure to one-time genetic treatments for cardiovascular disease. Ascidian gives Lilly access to RNA
exon-editing technology for rare inherited kidney diseases. Scorpion expands the oncology pipeline with a targeted cancer
program. Morphic adds oral integrin biology in inflammatory bowel disease. Insilico gives Lilly access to AI-native
discovery programs. Isomorphic provides exposure to frontier AI drug-design intelligence. Each deal adds a different type
of biological option.
Together, these moves show that Lilly is building across modalities, not just molecules.
That is important because the future of medicine may not be dominated by one therapeutic approach. It may be a portfolio
of programmable tools: peptides, antibodies, small molecules, RNA therapies, gene editors, cell therapies,
radiopharmaceuticals, and AI-designed combinations. Lilly’s M&A strategy gives it more shots on goal across that
expanding biological toolkit.
This is also where the ROIC argument becomes stronger. A company with a weak discovery engine can destroy value
through M&A by overpaying for assets it does not understand. A company with a strong biological intelligence engine may
be able to do the opposite. It can identify better targets earlier, understand fit more quickly, integrate assets more
effectively, and kill weaker programs before they consume too much capital.
That is why Lilly’s dealmaking should not be separated from LillyPod, TuneLab, Isomorphic, Insilico, and its internal data
moat. They are all part of the same strategy. Lilly is building the infrastructure to discover internally, learn externally,
partner selectively, acquire aggressively, and then push the best assets through one of the strongest clinical, regulatory,
manufacturing, and commercial machines in healthcare.
This is what makes the company so different from a small AI biotech. An AI-native startup may have a brilliant model, but
it usually lacks the capital, clinical infrastructure, manufacturing scale, regulatory experience, and global distribution
needed to turn a discovery into a medicine. Lilly has those assets already. The more intelligence it adds upstream, the
more valuable its downstream machine becomes.
8

 M&A is therefore confirmation of the thesis.
Lilly is using today’s metabolic cash flow to buy tomorrow’s biological optionality. It is turning GLP-1 profits into a broader
portfolio of AI, data, modalities, platforms, and therapeutic shots on goal. In a world where biology is becoming more
computational, that may be one of the highest-return uses of capital available.
The Consumer Is Learning the Language of Biology
There is another reason this analogy matters: consumers are beginning to understand it before investors fully do.
Search interest for “peptides” has surged as GLP-1s moved from a diabetes and obesity category into mainstream
culture. The word itself has crossed over from laboratories and clinical journals into TikTok, skincare, wellness clinics,
Reddit threads, celebrity weight-loss discussions, and anti-aging conversations. That is important because every major
platform shift requires a consumer translation layer. The Mag 7 did not become dominant only because developers
understood APIs. They became dominant because consumers adopted the products those APIs enabled: search,
smartphones, social graphs, cloud software, digital commerce, streaming, and now AI assistants. The technical layer
became economically powerful once the consumer layer made it visible.
Peptides may be entering a similar moment in biology.
GLP-1s have educated the public that a small biological signal can profoundly change appetite, metabolism, body
composition, and health outcomes. That awareness has created a halo around the broader peptide category. Some of it is
legitimate medicine. Some of it is skincare. Some of it is biohacking. Some of it is a regulatory gray area. But the cultural
signal is clear: consumers are starting to believe that biology is programmable.
That is the confirmation investors should pay attention to.
The consumer is no longer only asking for a drug to treat a disease. The consumer is asking for access to a pathway:
weight loss, recovery, muscle, sleep, skin quality, inflammation, fertility, cognition, and aging. In the digital era, the killer
app was access to information, commerce, communication, and software. In the biological era, the killer app may be
access to human performance, metabolic control, and healthspan.
This does not mean every peptide trend is safe, proven, or investable. In fact, the gray-market boom around unapproved
research peptides may be a warning that demand is moving faster than regulation and clinical evidence. But that is also
part of the signal. Consumer curiosity is outrunning the existing medical system. Whenever that happens at scale, a new
platform layer usually forms. The winners are rarely the unregulated edge players. They are the companies that can take
consumer demand, validate it clinically, protect it with intellectual property, manufacture it reliably, distribute it safely, and
wrap it inside data and AI feedback loops.
That is why Lilly’s position is so important. GLP-1s are generating revenue and teaching the market that peptides can be
mass-market biological interfaces. The consumer is learning the language of human software. Lilly is building the
infrastructure to industrialize it.
Competition Exists, but the Race Is Different
The paper I wrote last year was a broader one on pharma and Lilly absolutely has competitors. Novo Nordisk is a major
force in metabolic disease. Roche, Novartis, AstraZeneca, Sanofi, Merck, Pfizer, and many others are investing in AI.
Recursion, Isomorphic, Insilico, Xaira, and other AI-native companies are building important platforms. NVIDIA will partner
broadly. The field will be competitive.
But Lilly’s competition is not the same as the LLM race.
It is not a clean benchmark race where everyone trains broadly similar models and competes for the same general
intelligence layer. Lilly’s advantage is that its model is specialized, its data is proprietary, its workflows are internal, and its
capital comes from a massive operating business. Competitors can buy GPUs. They cannot easily buy Lilly’s historical
data. They cannot instantly recreate its therapeutic focus, manufacturing scale, clinical infrastructure, regulatory
9

 knowledge, or GLP-1 cash flow. They can build models, but they may not be able to build the same closed-loop biological
system.
This is where the investment story becomes powerful.
GLP-1 revenue is strategic fuel. Lilly can reinvest today’s profits into the infrastructure that may create tomorrow’s
medicines. Each new molecule, each failed experiment, each clinical trial, each manufacturing improvement, and each
partner interaction can become more data for the system.
The result is a compounding loop: GLP-1s generate cash; cash funds AI infrastructure; AI infrastructure improves
discovery; improved discovery expands the pipeline; the pipeline creates more data and revenue; the data improves the
models.
That is how a pharmaceutical company becomes an AI infrastructure company without investors fully noticing.
The market often struggles with this kind of transition because it tries to fit companies into old categories. Lilly is a pharma
company. NVIDIA is an AI chip company. OpenAI is a model company. But the future does not respect those categories.
NVIDIA is moving from chips to AI factories. OpenAI is moving from chat to agents. Lilly is moving from drugs to biological
intelligence.
The convergence is the story.
Conclusion: From Metabolic Cash Flow to Biological Intelligence
For Lilly, the GLP-1 franchise may be remembered in two ways. The first is obvious: as one of the largest therapeutic
breakthroughs in modern medicine. The second may prove even more important: as the financial bridge that allowed Lilly
to build a new kind of drug discovery machine.
Mounjaro and Zepbound are treating metabolic disease today, but the cash flow they generate may fund the AI systems
that discover future therapies for cardiovascular disease, neurodegeneration, cancer, immune dysfunction, genetic
disease, and aging-related decline.
That is the deeper meaning of the Lilly story.
This is about the possibility that the largest healthcare revenue engine of this decade is financing a specialized AI platform
for the next era of medicine.
The LLM companies are building general intelligence for the world of information. Lilly is building specialized intelligence
for the world of biology. The first race may change how we work. The second may change how we age.
That is why Lilly deserves to be viewed as a GLP-1 leader and one of the most important AI infrastructure companies in
healthcare. Its advantage is that the capital is arriving at the precise moment when biology is becoming computational,
agents are becoming scientific workers, consumers are learning that biological pathways can be accessed, and
proprietary data is becoming the new source of pharmaceutical power.
GLP-1s are funding the machine. AI is training the machine. Lilly’s data is differentiating the machine. TuneLab is
expanding the machine. Isomorphic and Insilico are extending the machine. Consumer demand is validating the machine.
And the long-term output may be a new industrial model for discovering, designing, manufacturing, and personalizing
medicine.
If the first phase of AI was about language, the next phase will be about life.
Lilly is positioning itself for that transition. The company’s GLP-1 franchise gave it the cash flow. NVIDIA gives it the
compute. TuneLab gives it the distributed learning network. Isomorphic gives it exposure to frontier drug-design
intelligence. Insilico gives it access to AI-native discovery programs. Agentic wet labs give it the feedback loop. Genetic

10

 medicine gives it the programmable frontier. Longevity gives it the ultimate market. And peptides give it the language of
access between artificial intelligence and the human operating system.
That is the thesis: Lilly is using the profits from the metabolic disease revolution to finance a Manhattan Project for
biological intelligence.
And if that system works, the company will be building the infrastructure through which future medicines are discovered.

The Economy Is Being Rewired Around Compute and Energy “And
that’s a very different world.”
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

Recently, I listened to a podcast with Raoul Pal and Julien Bittel on the macro world today titled Why the Global Economy
Is Changing Rapidly. Near the end of the conversation, they talked about something I have written about in bits and
pieces over the last couple of years. Because of the “this versus that” nature of the discussion, combined with all the
recent growing bubble talk on X, I thought it was important to share my thoughts on their conversation.
Early in the podcast, Raoul set the framework:
“I’ve had this whole thesis around this universal code that everything is funneling from energy into intelligence and it’s
driving the geopolitical process. It’s driving the investment process. It’s driving literally everything right now.”
Later, he added:
“And you know, we’re going to shift from the magic formula of GDP equals population growth plus debt growth plus
productivity growth into the economic singularity version, which is AI plus robots plus humans. And productivity is how
much intelligence you can produce per unit of energy.”
Then Julien summed it up:
“Yeah. We’re leaving a world that’s been dependent on capital and labor and moving into a world that’s entirely dependent
on compute and energy. And that’s a very different world.”
This gets to the heart of why we are watching something that has never happened before. Experienced people keep
saying they have seen this AI playbook before and that it ends badly. I understand the instinct, because every major
investment cycle eventually has excess, disappointment, and corrections. But this is also different. We are watching the
industrial world change in front of our eyes, and with it the cycles most of our models and memories were built on.
To summarize what is happening as billions of digital employees, and soon humanoids, enter the workforce: we exited the
old economic formula of capital, labor, population, debt, and productivity, and entered a new world where compute and
energy become the foundation of growth, the base of the five-layer cake, because productivity is now measured by how
much intelligence can be produced per unit of energy.
For most of modern economic history, the business cycle has been understood through the tension between labor and
capital. Companies borrowed money, hired workers, built capacity, and expanded until demand slowed, margins
compressed, asset prices fell, and the debt that financed the expansion became the problem. When the cycle turned,
labor became the release valve. Companies cut workers to protect levered balance sheets, reduce costs, and repair the
damage created by overinvestment. That was the textbook cycle: capital overreached, labor absorbed the shock, and
credit transmitted the pain.
The AI cycle is following a different model. It is the reindustrialization of intelligence, and its constraints are increasingly
compute and energy.

 Jensen Huang has described AI as a five-layer cake: energy, chips, infrastructure, models, and applications. That framing
matters because the bottom of the stack is physical. Before there are agents, copilots, digital workers, enterprise
automation, robotics, or intelligence-as-a-service, there must be electricity, semiconductors, memory, networking, cooling,
land, power interconnections, and data centers. AI may feel like software to the user, but economically it begins as
industry. It starts with atoms before it becomes bits. Huang’s other formulation is even simpler: the input is electrons, the
output is tokens. That is the new production function. The factory consumes energy and produces intelligence.
This is also why the AI cycle is so different from the software cycle that preceded it. The irony is that the great software
winners already showed us that the labor-capital relationship was breaking. It is what drove me from spending a month of
the year in China to visiting Singularity University more than a decade ago to update my macro framework to be based on
exponential innovation. Microsoft, Google, Meta, Amazon, and other platform companies scaled revenue, margins, and
market value without scaling labor or brick-and-mortar locations in the old industrial way. The opposite of ghost cities.
Revenue per employee and profit margins for the leading technology companies moved into numbers that would have
been unimaginable in traditional manufacturing, retail, or transportation.
The software world proved that once a platform is built, additional revenue can arrive with very little incremental
headcount. That was the first crack in the old model. It is one of the reasons why, since the Great Financial Crisis, there
has not been a credit and labor led recession. The only recession: the government-driven Covid shutdown. AI widens this
crack even further. If software allowed companies to scale with fewer people, AI allows companies to scale with a digital
labor force that works 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, 365 days a year. The age of relying on historical economic
analogies is breaking, and so are the memories and patterns investors associate with bubbles and investment cycles.
Tokens are the food source of these digital workers. A human employee consumes wages, benefits, office space,
management time, and training. Then they have families, buy cars, have children, and leverage up through a home. A
digital employee consumes compute, memory, electricity, and data. That changes the bottleneck, and it also changes the
business cycle. In the old economy, a company needed more workers to produce more output. In the AI economy, a
company needs more tokens. More tokens require more compute. More compute requires more energy. Labor still
matters, but the marginal unit of growth is changing. The next unit of productivity is less likely to be a newly hired person
and more likely to be a software agent running on infrastructure somewhere in Virginia, Texas, Arizona, Ohio, Taiwan, or
Ireland. For human employees, the dream of the rising the corporate ladder has turned into the anxiety of AI driven job
losses. This leaves the stock market at all-time highs and consumer sentiment suggesting we are in a recession.
That is why today’s AI capital spending should not be analyzed like a normal credit bubble. In prior cycles, excess
borrowing funded excess capacity, and when demand disappointed, the debt remained. Investors are understandably
focused on the size of the debt being raised, the amount of free cash flow being consumed, and whether the capex can
earn an adequate return. Those questions matter. But in the old cycle, the first place we looked was debt-to-equity,
refinancing risk, and whether companies had borrowed beyond the capacity of their balance sheets. This cycle looks
different. These companies have the ability to raise hundreds of billions of dollars precisely because their balance sheets,
income statements, market positions, and cash flows give them access to capital on a scale that highly levered industrial
borrowers in prior cycles never had. The hyperscalers are spending hundreds of billions of dollars on AI infrastructure, but
they are highly profitable businesses with dominant market positions, enormous cash flows, and balance sheets that look
nothing like the overextended borrowers of prior investment booms.
They also have over $1 trillion, and growing, in cloud receivables for the world’s digital workforce. Demand is already far
greater than supply. Demand grows daily in the form of bits, while compute is dependent on the complexity of atoms.
Every dollar of AI capex still has to earn an adequate return, and some of it will disappoint. The real risk is that investors
keep using the wrong historical template.
What is also different is that many of the companies building the infrastructure and intelligence stack are increasingly
coming to public markets to raise capital, and investors continue to absorb the supply. Google recently came to the market
and raised an astonishing $85 billion in equity, with Berkshire Hathaway purchasing roughly $10 billion despite the stock
having doubled over the prior year. Meta has leaked its desire to tap the public equity markets. Behind Google and Meta
sits an extraordinary pipeline of potential issuers. OpenAI, Anthropic, and SpaceX alone could eventually bring more than
$4 trillion of combined market capitalization to public markets, with the money funding even more capex.

2

 In a traditional cycle, investors might struggle to finance that scale of issuance. Instead, capital continues to flow toward
the companies building the compute, energy, infrastructure, and intelligence layers of the AI economy. All of this supply is
arriving while the broader stock market continues to move higher, a sign of how powerful the demand for ownership in the
AI buildout has become.
At the same time, the household transmission mechanism has also changed. Despite how often you hear that very few
people benefit from the rising stock market, the facts are that roughly two-thirds of Americans now own stocks directly or
indirectly, most often through retirement accounts and pension funds. That means a rising stock market does more than
enrich a narrow owner class; it also supports household confidence for short term expenditures, retirement balances, and
consumption, even if the savings rate goes down.
On the other side of the distribution, government transfers have become a much larger share of personal income than
they were fifty years ago. The result is a strange form of economic stabilization also caused by exponential innovation
through its Schumpeterian process. Asset appreciation supports one large part of the population, while transfer payments
support another. This does not solve wealth concentration or inequality, and it may even worsen resentment, but it helps
explain why the economy can keep absorbing shocks without falling into the old recession pattern. Unless there is a large
wave of layoffs from rapidly declining profit margins, the traditional business-cycle trigger is harder to find.
The better place to look for a supply demand cycle and mismatch is the AI adoption cycle colliding with a physical
bottleneck. Human adoption and enterprise adoption are still at the very early stage. ChatGPT reaching massive global
usage in only a few years showed how fast the demand side can move when intelligence becomes both a consumer and
enterprise interface. Anthropic’s recent parabolic rise in ARR confirms it. But enterprise AI is still uneven. Many companies
are being forced to accelerate plans because of competition. They are experimenting, and fewer have rewired workflows.
Only a minority have scaled AI deeply across their organizations.
That means the demand for intelligence, along with the capex cycle, is still in the early innings even as the supply chain
already looks strained. This is the heart of the new cycle: bits can compound almost instantly, but atoms cannot. Models,
apps, agents, and demand can grow exponentially. Power plants, transmission lines, transformers, fabs, substations, and
data centers cannot.
This is where we stand today. The bottlenecks and shortages across the physical AI supply chain are not easing. They are
spreading. Last year, the conversation was mostly about transformers, grid connections, gas turbines, and power
availability. At the time, memory still looked relatively abundant. Now the shortages have moved deeper into the compute
stack. We are hearing about tighter supply in memory, CPUs, advanced packaging, networking equipment, and the power
components needed to support higher-density racks.
That matters because it shows this is not one isolated constraint. It is a rolling shortage cycle across the entire physical
layer of AI. As soon as one bottleneck is identified, demand pushes into the next constraint. The market may reprice the
winners and losers along the way, but the broader message is clear: the digital side of AI can scale demand faster than
the industrial side can scale supply. Then if you add in the back-to-back years of commodity and mineral shortages due to
rare earth trade wars and the shutting of the Strait of Hormuz you have more warning signs around the supply chain.
Petrochemicals, silver and copper are also necessary for the AI buildout and are all under strains as well.
AI does not eliminate corrections, risk, overinvestment, or disappointment. There will still be investment corrections. There
will still be moments when expectations run ahead of reality. There will still be periods when markets have to reprice the
timing, profitability, and scale of the buildout. But in the AI cycle, I believe those corrections will be less about the old
credit-driven relationship between labor and capital and more about bottlenecks and shortages in the physical world.
The demand side is being driven by digital employees that can work continuously and consume more intelligence every
day. The supply side is constrained by atoms: power plants, chips, memory, transformers, cooling systems, transmission
lines, and data centers. That mismatch is the new cycle. The risk is real, but it is different. It is the risk that hypergrowth in
digital intelligence runs faster than the physical world can feed it. During those times, expect to see major volatility in the
AI infrastructure trades as investors lose clarity on when the earnings and ROIC can come through relative to the speed
and growth of the capex spend.

3

 That is the reality of the cycle we have entered and where we are likely to be for a long time. The old cycle asked whether
labor costs were rising too fast, whether credit was too loose, whether companies had borrowed too much, and whether
demand would fall enough to force layoffs. It was built around capital versus human labor. The AI cycle asks different
questions. How do we feed the digital employees? Is there enough power? Are there enough chips? Is there enough
memory? Can data centers get connected to the grid? Can the physical infrastructure keep pace with the demand for
intelligence? Can enterprises adopt AI fast enough to justify the buildout, and can the buildout happen fast enough to
satisfy adoption?
There are no textbooks for this because the economy has never reindustrialized intelligence before. The business cycle is
no longer simply labor versus capital. Increasingly, it is compute versus energy and as Raoul and Julien said, “And that’s a
very different world.”

4
