156494:
June 14, 2026

Entegris Update: Vera Rubin Confirms the Missing Chemical Layer of
AI Complexity
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

What Has Changed Since the Original Entegris Spotlight
The original Entegris paper was built around the missing chemical layer of the AI economy. Semiconductors were getting
most of the attention, but the real-world manufacturing stack required a hidden layer of high-purity chemicals, filtration,
CMP, deposition materials, and contamination control.
Nothing has changed with that thought.
What has changed is that Vera Rubin is giving investors and analysts a more specific map of where the complexity is
going. The next stage of AI infrastructure is not just about adding more accelerators. It is about designing rack-scale
systems where compute, memory, networking, storage, and packaging are increasingly integrated.
That complexity reality is the main story because every one of those design choices increases the importance of
materials science.
More HBM means more advanced memory manufacturing and packaging intensity. More memory bandwidth means
tighter process tolerances. More local storage means NAND becomes more strategic to the AI system. More advanced
packaging means more bonding, cleaning, CMP, underfills, substrates, and thermal-interface materials. More dense racks
mean more thermal and reliability challenges.
The update is that Vera Rubin is making Entegris’s opportunity more visible and the chart of the stock as it attempts alltime new highs shows investors are jumping on board.
Why Entegris Is Important to the AI Buildout
This year, the broadening out theme in semiconductors away from Nvidia has been the alpha generator. This is not
speculation or a bubble, this is the reality that we have transitioned away from GPUs. Beginning with CES in January,
Jensen Huang has prepared the market for the transition to tokens per watt and Vera Rubin. For this transtion the
bottlenecks are increasingly underneath it: memory, packaging, interconnect, thermals, substrate capacity, yield, and
contamination control.
Entegris sits in those lower layers.
The company monetizes complexity, not just volume. Its opportunity grows when wafers require more process steps,
tighter contamination thresholds, more filtration, more specialty chemicals, more deposition, more selective etch, more
CMP, more cleaning, and more advanced packaging materials.
That makes Entegris relevant to AI because every major AI semiconductor trend increases manufacturing complexity.
Leading-edge logic requires tighter purity and more advanced materials. HBM requires more CMP, more advanced
packaging, more bonding, more thermal management, and more process control. NAND layer scaling requires more etch,

 deposition, cleans, filtration, and contamination control. Advanced packaging adds a new process layer that is
increasingly central to AI system performance.
The old semiconductor question was: how many wafers are being started?
The better AI-era question is: how much complexity is embedded in each wafer?
That is where Entegris benefits. If NAND moves to higher layer counts, Entegris content per wafer rises. If DRAM moves
deeper into HBM and hybrid bonding, Entegris content rises. If logic moves to more advanced nodes, Entegris content
rises. If advanced packaging becomes the center of AI performance, Entegris content rises.
The company is important because AI is shifting the semiconductor value chain from simple unit growth to materials
intensity. Entegris is one of the companies that helps turn AI demand into manufacturable silicon.
Why Vera Rubin Makes the Theme More Timely
Vera Rubin is the complexity accelerant because it shows the next phase of AI infrastructure becoming more memoryintensive, more storage-intensive, more packaging-intensive, and more thermally constrained.
This is not a small change. It is a change in the physical recipe of the AI system.
The first implication is HBM intensity. Vera Rubin pushes the industry further into HBM4 and higher memory bandwidth.
HBM is not just a memory product. It is a stacked, packaging-intensive, yield-sensitive architecture. It requires more wafer
capacity, more bonding, more CMP, more cleaning, more filtration, and more process control than conventional memory.
The second implication is NAND intensity. The market still tends to treat NAND as the lagging memory recovery. But Vera
Rubin suggests NAND is becoming more strategic to AI systems. As GPUs require faster access to larger data sets, more
local storage, and tighter coordination between compute and storage, NAND becomes less of a commodity appendage
and more of a system-level requirement.
For Entegris, that matters because NAND is a content-per-wafer story. More layers mean more high-aspect-ratio etch,
more deposition, more cleaning, more filtration, and more opportunities for contamination to destroy value. Even if wafer
starts are not booming, higher-layer NAND can still increase Entegris content per wafer.
The third implication is advanced packaging. Vera Rubin is a rack-scale system. It is not just a chip. Performance comes
from how efficiently the system moves data between GPUs, CPUs, memory, storage, and networking. That makes
advanced packaging and interconnect part of the core AI performance stack.
That is exactly where Entegris matters. Hybrid bonding, underfills, substrates, thermal-interface materials, CMP, cleaning,
and contamination control become more important as the package becomes more complex and more valuable.
The fourth implication is process churn. The move from Blackwell to Vera Rubin to Rubin Ultra to future architectures
means the AI supply chain is entering a faster cadence of requalification, new materials, new package designs, and new
yield challenges. For a toll-taker like Entegris, that churn is valuable. Every new process and new material must be
qualified, controlled, cleaned, filtered, and protected.
That is the timely update. Vera Rubin makes the missing chemical layer more visible because the next AI architecture
depends even more heavily on the exact bottlenecks Entegris serves.
The NAND Surprise: Storage Moves Closer to the AI System
The most underappreciated part of the Vera Rubin update may be NAND.
The consensus view is that NAND is a late-cycle recovery story. Demand improves, pricing recovers, and layer scaling
resumes. That is true, but it is not the full story.

2

 The bigger surprise is that AI is pulling NAND into the performance architecture.
As models get larger and inference workloads become more complex, the system needs faster access to larger data sets
and more efficient movement between storage, memory, and compute. That means NAND may become less like cheap
storage sitting outside the system and more like part of the AI memory hierarchy.
This changes the quality of NAND demand. If NAND becomes more closely tied to GPUs, high-bandwidth storage, or
advanced AI system architectures, then the manufacturing bar rises. More advanced NAND, higher layer counts, and
tighter packaging flows would all increase the materials and purity burden.
For Entegris, this is important because NAND layer scaling is one of the cleanest examples of content-per-wafer growth.
More layers mean more etch, more deposition, more cleans, more CMP, and more filtration. A higher-layer NAND wafer
can be more valuable to Entegris even without a dramatic increase in wafer starts.
In other words, Vera Rubin may turn NAND from a cyclical recovery footnote into a structural AI materials story.
HBM and Advanced Packaging: The Purity Wall
HBM is the easiest place to see the Entegris opportunity.
AI systems are increasingly memory-bandwidth constrained. HBM solves that problem, but it does so by making memory
manufacturing and packaging much harder. Stacked memory, through-silicon vias, wafer thinning, hybrid bonding, and
advanced packaging all create more surfaces to clean, more layers to planarize, more residues to remove, and more
opportunities for defects.
This creates a purity wall.
As the value of the package rises, the cost of failure rises with it. A small contamination event can destroy an extremely
expensive component. That makes filtration, purification, CMP, cleaning, and process materials less discretionary. They
become yield insurance.
This is the central Entegris thesis. The company benefits when the industry cannot afford defects.
Vera Rubin raises the stakes because it increases the memory and packaging content of the AI system. The more
valuable and complex the package becomes, the more valuable Entegris’s role becomes.
Physical AI Extends the Duration
Vera Rubin is not just a data-center story. It also points toward the broader future of physical AI.
As AI moves from centralized cloud systems into edge devices, humanoids, autonomous vehicles, drones, industrial
robots, and eventually orbital data centers, the number of intelligent machines in the world rises. Each of those machines
requires some combination of compute, memory, sensors, storage, power semiconductors, connectivity, packaging, and
thermal management.
This is important for Entegris because it broadens the demand base.
Edge AI may not always use the most advanced nodes, but it increases the number of chips that need to be
manufactured reliably. Humanoids are essentially mobile AI systems with sensors, power electronics, memory, compute,
motors, batteries, and thermal constraints. AI full self-driving vehicles are sensor-rich edge data centers on wheels.
Drones and autonomous systems are distributed AI nodes operating in the physical world.
The content per wafer will vary. Some of this silicon will sit on trailing nodes, where Entegris content is lower. But the
direction is clear: AI is increasing the semiconductor and materials intensity of the physical economy.

3

 Orbital data centers are the long-tail version of the same idea. They are not a near-term revenue driver for Entegris, but
they are a signal that compute demand is running into physical constraints on Earth: power, land, cooling, and permitting.
Space-based compute also requires radiation hardening, hermetic packaging, novel thermal solutions, and extremely high
reliability. That is a specialty-materials problem.
The near-term Entegris thesis is data centers, HBM, NAND, advanced logic, and advanced packaging. The long-term
thesis is that AI moves into everything physical.
The Investment Setup
The market’s mistake is likely to be treating Entegris as a cyclical semiconductor consumables name tied mostly to capex
and wafer starts.
The better frame is that Entegris is becoming a structural beneficiary of AI manufacturing complexity.
If wafer starts rise, Entegris wins.
If content per wafer rises, Entegris wins.
If NAND adds more layers, Entegris wins.
If HBM consumes more wafer capacity and packaging intensity, Entegris wins.
If advanced packaging becomes the center of AI system performance, Entegris wins.
If AI moves into cars, robots, drones, edge devices, and orbital systems, Entegris gains a broader demand base.
This is the power of the model. Entegris does not need every AI end market to work at once. It needs intelligence to keep
moving into more silicon, and for that silicon to keep getting more complex.
Vera Rubin makes that trend more visible.
Earnings Commentary
Management’s own commentary reinforces why the timing of this update matters. In Q4, Dave Reeder said customers are
adopting “more complex device architectures” to support “the rapid growth of AI,” creating “a broad set of opportunities for
Entegris,” and he tied that directly to “higher content per wafer.” On that same call, management said Entegris was
“gaining traction in DRAM and HBM,” especially in “CMP consumables and advanced packaging solutions,” while also
winning across “next-generation NAND” in deposition materials, CMP, and selective etch. The importance of NAND is
reinforced by management’s own language: across the last two earnings commentaries, Entegris mentioned NAND 39
times, underscoring that NAND is no longer just a cyclical memory recovery story but an increasingly important materialsintensity driver tied to AI storage, layer scaling, and content per wafer.
The Q1 commentary then sharpened the point: management said process complexity at advanced nodes is driving higher
Entegris content per wafer, that the memory market is “structurally strong” because AI workloads are reshaping DRAM
and NAND architectures, and that NAND demand is now supported not only by technology transitions but by “AI-driven
storage requirements.” In other words, the company is saying the same thing this paper is arguing: AI complexity is
moving directly into Entegris’s highest-value lanes.
Conclusion
The original Entegris thesis was that high-purity chemicals and materials science are the missing layer of the AI economy.
The Vera Rubin update is that this missing layer is becoming more important, not less.

4

 Vera Rubin shows that the next phase of AI infrastructure is more memory-intensive, more storage-intensive, more
packaging-intensive, more thermally constrained, and more dependent on manufacturing precision. That is exactly the
environment where Entegris’s value rises.
The cleanest investment conclusion is this:
Entegris is not a bet on semiconductor units. It is a bet on the rising materials complexity required to
manufacture intelligence.
AI does not just need more chips. It needs more yield, more purity, more filtration, more CMP, more deposition, more etch,
more packaging, more memory, and more advanced materials.
Vera Rubin confirms that the AI buildout is moving deeper into Entegris’s lane.

June 15, 2026

The AI Mid-Cycle Slowdown: From Building Intelligence to Applying It
Jordi Visser
JVisser@22VResearch.com

From Discovery to Digestion
In macro, a mid-cycle slowdown is the pause inside an expansion. Growth is still positive and the cycle is still intact, but
the rate of change cools, the early leaders get expensive, and investors trade an easy question for a harder one. The
question shifts from “is this real?” to “what do I pay for the next dollar of it?” AI just ran through its early-cycle catch-up at a
speed no normal economy could match. We went from skepticism to acceptance to a full capital rush in roughly six
months. That catch-up is now behind us. What comes next looks a lot like a classic mid-cycle slowdown. The story is still
powerful, but the easy money in the obvious trade is no longer sitting on the table.
The clearest evidence of how far the early cycle carried us is the cadence of the models themselves. The releases have
been arriving faster than the market can digest them, each one landing before the last has been fully priced. That cadence
culminated last week in Fable 5, the most capable model ever made broadly available, posting state-of-the-art results
across software engineering, scientific research, vision, and long-horizon agentic work. Then, late Friday, it was pulled
back and restricted on national-security grounds. A commercial product was released to the public and then partially
withdrawn. Regardless of the reason, this is not a normal software cycle. It is one of the clearest markers we have of how
far the underlying technology has traveled, and it tells you the binding constraint in this market is no longer whether the
models are good enough. They are.
The Model Curve Keeps Moving
It is worth being precise about where this model progress came from, because it changes how you handicap the next leg.
A meaningful share of the recent jump in capability has come from algorithmic and post-training advances: explicit
reasoning and test-time compute, reinforcement learning in its various forms, including RLHF and the newer push into
reinforcement learning on verifiable rewards and AI feedback, distillation, and better, increasingly synthetic training data.
The models got smarter by thinking longer and being trained better, rather than merely being trained bigger.
That is the setup for what comes next, because we have spent this stretch improving the software of intelligence while the
next generation of hardware was still being built. Now both arrive at once. Blackwell is shipping in volume, the Vera Rubin
platform has just entered production, and Rubin Ultra sits on the 2027 roadmap behind it, all of it feeding far larger data
centers. Stack continued algorithmic progress on top of that step-change in compute and the model curve should keep
climbing. In my view, it points toward something that looks like Einstein-level problem-solving sooner than investors are
positioned for. That is the whole case for the application layer. Every gain in the underlying models flows downhill to
whoever turns them into products.
Two Horizons
It is worth separating two horizons, because they trade very differently. Einstein-level intelligence is more than a sharper
answer to a question you already know how to ask. Paired with agents that can plan, use tools, and act with minimal
supervision, it becomes something closer to autonomous problem-solving: systems that can take an open-ended,
genuinely hard problem and work it toward a solution largely on their own. That is the disruptive frontier, and it is
enormous. Whole categories of progress that have been rate-limited by the scarcity of human genius, in materials, drug
design, engineering, and the harder corners of finance, could come unstuck in a way that reorders entire industries.

 This is also where a handicapper has to stay honest about the odds. That frontier is uncertain in both timing and shape. It
will mint winners and losers the market cannot price today, and it is a multi-year disruption rather than a clean near-term
trade. So I hold it as the bigger call to position into as it arrives, rather than the one to underwrite right now. For the next
year, the part you can actually model is more workmanlike and more bankable: productivity. Enterprise adoption,
efficiency, and margin gains are finally beginning to show up in real financials. Get paid for the productivity wave that is
already here, and stay positioned for the problem-solving disruption that is coming behind it.
There is a sting in the tail of this for the hardware trade, too. The faster intelligence improves through software, the louder
the question grows of whether every announced dollar of capex is really needed. Cheaper, more efficient models also
tend to set off price wars that commoditize the very intelligence the buildout was meant to produce, leading to a shift to the
edge. That does not mean the data centers stop getting built. Demand for compute can keep climbing even as the price of
a token falls. It means pricing power and margin migrate away from manufacturing raw intelligence and toward applying it.
That is one more reason the easy money has left the obvious trade and the durable value is moving up the stack.
Infrastructure Endures, But the Basket Trade Matures
That brings us to the part of the trade that is now fully understood, though understood is not the same as finished. The
infrastructure buildout, the bottom of Jensen Huang’s five-layer cake, was the early-cycle winner for good reason.
Demand was real, compute was scarce, backlogs were visible, and earnings revisions did the heavy lifting. None of that is
a secret anymore. Here is the part the bears get wrong. In absolute terms this buildout is still in the early innings. The
scale of what has to be constructed in power, data centers, and silicon over the coming years is so large that the theme
does not roll over. It endures.
What changes in a mid-cycle slowdown is the rate of change and the texture of the trade. Growth decelerates off a torrid
pace, the tape gets rockier, and the one-decision basket stops doing the work for you. I expect the tight correlations that
carried the whole complex higher to break down. Names and sub-themes that moved together on the way up will start to
diverge, and leadership will rotate within the buildout as the bottlenecks themselves keep migrating from raw compute
toward power, cooling, optical, memory, and advanced packaging. Each bottleneck hands the baton to a different set of
winners. The capital intensity is rising, and the financing is increasingly showing up as large debt and equity raises rather
than as free cash flow thrown off by the business. The bar for earnings has moved from beating estimates to beating and
raising, or getting punished. So the message is not to abandon the infrastructure theme. I still remain steadfast in my view
that this is a multi-year rotation from the spenders to the infrastructure receivers. The message is that the receiver theme
has matured from an easy basket directional trade into more of a stockpicker’s market.
From Building Intelligence to Applying It
Here is the synthesis the mid-cycle forces on you. If the models are now this good, and if the agentic world has officially
arrived, then the scarce resource is no longer simply the factory that produces intelligence. It is also not raw intelligence
itself, which is likely to get cheaper over time. The scarce resource becomes the trusted application of intelligence to solve
problems: proprietary data, workflow integration, distribution, identity, settlement, domain expertise, and real-world
outcomes that customers can actually rely on.
The first phase of AI funded the compute, the data centers, the power, and the model labs. The next phase has to prove
the return on all of that spending, and that proof lives in the application layer that has lagged the entire way up. My base
case for the next year is that this is where the market’s attention migrates. As enterprise adoption broadens from pilots to
production, the productivity benefits stop being a slide in a keynote and start showing up in real company financials: in
margins, in headcount that no longer grows in lockstep with revenue, and in work that gets done faster and at lower cost.
That is the moment the application layer stops being a promise and becomes a print. Markets pay for prints.
Within that application layer, two areas I have talked about have my focus. The first is drug discovery. The same
generation of models that got restricted for being too powerful is also the first to generate novel, testable scientific
hypotheses at scale. That capability points straight at the lab, where the prize is owning the compression of time and cost
involved in finding a molecule that actually works and proving it in the clinic. Healthcare around it benefits in the same
motion, from diagnostics to clinical workflow to trial design to the administrative back office that swallows a third of every
dollar spent on care. These are domains where specialized AI applied to proprietary, hard-to-replicate data produces
outcomes rather than just throughput, and outcomes are what enterprises will pay for.
2

 More broadly, this is where the agentic shift earns its keep: software where agents execute multi-step work instead of
autocompleting a sentence, financial and back-office automation, legal, compliance, and customer operations. The reason
this is investable now and was not eighteen months ago is simple. The models finally clear the capability bar, and agents
can finally act rather than merely suggest. That is the difference between a demo and a deployment, and deployment is
where measurable enterprise value gets created and, eventually, captured.
The Financial Guardrails of the Agentic Economy
The second area is the financial guardrails of the agentic economy, an application layer that almost nobody files under
applications. The common thread between drug discovery and crypto is not that they look similar on the surface. It is that
both sit where agentic intelligence leaves the chat box and enters the real world. In science, agents need to generate,
test, and validate hypotheses. In finance, agents need to authenticate, transact, settle, and prove permission. Both require
guardrails.
Knowing the skepticism on this crypto angle, I will begin a new YouTube weekly after the summer to coincide with my
belief that broad investor focus will move here the following year driven by the rise of tokenization and the parabolic
growth of the agentic economy. Once these Einstein-level IQ AI agents begin to transact, authenticate, and coordinate on
their own, they need rails for settlement, identity, and trust that do not depend on a human clicking approve. That is the
native use case for crypto infrastructure, stablecoins, and programmable settlement, which is why I treat these monetary
guardrails as part of the application layer rather than as a separate macro bet.
On timing, consider the line widely attributed to Charlie Munger: if all you ever did was buy quality when it traded down to
its 200-week moving average, you would do very well over time, and almost no one has the discipline to do it. Bitcoin
tagged its 200-week moving average for the first time this cycle in early June, near $61,000, with sentiment about as
washed out as it gets. That line has marked the major bottoms before, with 2022 the honest exception, so I am not
promising it holds. I am pointing out that the financial layer of the agentic economy is going on sale at the exact moment
the crowd has given up on it. That is usually when the handicapper starts paying attention.
The Transition
That is the whole mid-cycle thesis in a single frame. The boom is not ending. The buildout is not ending. The leadership is
rotating. It is rotating within the infrastructure trade as the old correlations break and new names take the baton. It is also
rotating over the next year toward the application layer built on top of it. Capital chased the easiest part of the story first:
the chips, the power, and the scarcity. That was what investors could see and underwrite.
The next phase moves toward the parts of the stack where the models, now extraordinary and now agentic, produce
measurable value in the real economy, with drug discovery and the financial guardrails of the agentic economy at the top
of my list. That is the transition from discovery to digestion, and from digestion to application. The fireworks phase is likely
behind us. The more durable phase is the one that has to pay for everything that came before it.
